title: Coinbase launching tokenized US stocks, backed 1:1, with holders able to receive dividends
blurb: Coinbase will launch tokenized US stocks backed 1:1 by real shares, with token holders eligible to receive dividends. The move puts Coinbase alongside rivals Robinhood and Kraken, as well as traditional financial institutions, in the race to bring equities trading on-chain.

title: What’s new with Unity Catalog at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks introduced a set of Unity Catalog updates at Data + AI Summit 2026, led by the Unity AI Gateway, which extends governance from data access to the runtime actions agents take across models, tools, and MCP servers. New additions include Glossary and Domains for shared business context, cross-cloud and cross-region addressability, and a centralized Governance Hub, now used by more than 14,000 organizations.

title: AWS WAF adds AI traffic monetization capability to help content owners charge AI bots for content access
blurb: AWS WAF added an AI traffic monetization capability that lets publishers charge AI bots and agents per request for content access, set by path, bot category, or verification tier without changing origin infrastructure. The Bot Control feature collects payments in stablecoins through Coinbase's x402 Facilitator, with Stripe and Machine Payments Protocol support coming, as AI crawlers now exceed 50% of web traffic for many providers.

title: Public and Private Medical Community Targeted by China-Nexus Threat Actor Pursuing Artificial Intelligence, Cyber, Medical, and National Defense Research
blurb: Google's Threat Intelligence Group disclosed a year-long campaign by UNC6508, a China-nexus actor that breached North American academic, medical, and military research institutions starting in September 2023. The group exploited public-facing REDCap servers, deployed custom INFINITERED malware, and exfiltrated data on AI, uncrewed vehicles, offensive cyber programs, and national defense before Google disrupted its infrastructure and notified affected organizations.

title: Java's Project Valhalla finally lands a preview in JDK 28
blurb: Java's Project Valhalla reached its first preview in JDK 28, introducing value classes that combine the performance of primitives with the abstraction of objects. Architect Brian Goetz cautioned that the feature will likely remain in preview through the next long-term support release, continuing a development effort that has run for over a decade.

title: Agent Bricks: Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Agent Bricks into a full developer agent platform at its Data + AI Summit 2026, letting developers build agents with any model or harness while Databricks handles infrastructure like sandboxes, memory, token capacity, deployment, and monitoring. The platform now supports OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Qwen, and Kimi models, plus a new SpaceX partnership to offer Grok, after users built 100k+ agents processing over 1 quadrillion tokens a year.

title: The Value Axis: Language Models Encode Whether They're on the Right Track
blurb: A study finds language models internally encode a "value" axis tracking whether their current strategy is succeeding, and steering it suppresses or induces self-correction.

title: Improvement in advanced Alzheimer’s disease following high-dose psilocybin
blurb: A case report describes cognitive and functional improvement in a patient with advanced Alzheimer's disease after treatment with high-dose psilocybin. The authors document gains in memory, mood, and daily functioning following the psychedelic compound, though a single uncontrolled case cannot establish efficacy and the findings will require controlled trials to validate.

title: Coinbase to join tokenized stock race with onchain shares, dividend payments
blurb: Coinbase announced plans to launch tokenized stocks backed one-for-one by underlying U.S. equities, letting users own, trade, and redeem shares onchain while automatically collecting dividends. CEO Brian Armstrong framed the products as direct equity ownership rather than the derivatives or synthetic exposure of existing offerings, with the rollout starting in eligible jurisdictions outside the U.S. on an unannounced date.

title: Databricks Sales Double for Product Rivaling Snowflake
blurb: Databricks said its data warehousing business more than doubled over the past year, reaching a $1.5B annual run rate. The product competes directly with Snowflake and Google in cloud data warehousing, pushing the privately held company deeper into a market where its analytics and AI tooling now overlap with established rivals.

title: A Billion Dollars of Leveraged SpaceX Bets Hit ETFs in One Day
blurb: Newly launched leveraged ETFs tied to SpaceX traded more than $1 billion in volume on their first day, as retail investors rushed to amplify wagers on the biggest IPO in history. The frenzy underscores extraordinary demand around Elon Musk's newest public company, with traders using the funds to take magnified positions on its post-listing swings.

title: Your "Pro" LLM Subscription May Actually Be "Free": Exposing Fingerprint Spoofing Risks in LLM Inference Services
blurb: A new attack, GhostPrint, lets malicious LLM providers serve a cheaper fine-tuned model that evades black-box fingerprinting meant to verify premium models.

title: BlackRock's new bitcoin ETF lets institutions earn from volatility. There's a catch.
blurb: BlackRock is expected to debut the iShares Bitcoin Premium Income ETF (BITA), which holds shares of its spot IBIT fund and sells call options against them to generate income from bitcoin's volatility. The covered-call strategy targets a 15% annual yield while capping upside at roughly 70% participation, and systematic call-writing at scale will likely push bitcoin's implied volatility lower still.

title: State Street targets stablecoin reserve boom with new money market fund
blurb: State Street launched a government money market fund built specifically for stablecoin issuers, joining BlackRock, Franklin Templeton, Fidelity and JPMorgan in the race to manage the Treasury bills and cash backing digital dollars. With global stablecoin issuance projected to reach $4 trillion by 2030, asset managers increasingly see reserve management as a fast-growing source of fee revenue rather than a crypto sideshow.

title: Ripple buys into Flutterwave Series E at $3.2 billion valuation, backs RLUSD integration
blurb: Ripple invested in Flutterwave's Series E at a $3.2 billion valuation, anchoring a deal to embed its RLUSD stablecoin and the XRP Ledger into the African payments firm's infrastructure across 34 countries. Flutterwave, which has processed over $50 billion across more than 1 billion transactions, will use the XRP Ledger for faster cross-border clearing and RLUSD as a settlement asset in its remittance corridors.

title: Databricks unveils CustomerLake, its agentic CDP
blurb: Databricks announced CustomerLake, an agentic customer data platform, at its Data + AI Summit, marking its entry into the martech market. Built on the company's lakehouse architecture and governed by Unity Catalog, the product consolidates identity resolution, audience building, and campaign activation into one platform, deploying agents that analyze behavior and act on customer data in real time.

title: Geometric Action Model for Robot Policy Learning
blurb: This paper introduces the Geometric Action Model (GAM), a language-conditioned robot manipulation policy that repurposes a pretrained geometric foundation model as a shared backbone for perception, future prediction, and action decoding. By splitting the model at an intermediate layer and forecasting future 3D latent tokens, GAM makes explicit the spatial geometry that prior vision-language-action models leave implicit, targeting contact-rich tasks where 2D representations fall short.

title: Task-Error Residual Learning for Real-Robot Five-Ball Juggling
blurb: A new method trained Barrett WAM robot arms to stably juggle three, four, and five balls by replacing reinforcement learning's scalar reward with directional task-error supervision, which packs more information into each attempt. The system converges from the second try, dropping only the first ball before task error falls monotonically, a feat that typically takes humans years of practice to master.

title: Claude Corps
blurb: Anthropic launched Claude Corps, a national fellowship that will train 1,000 fellows to use Claude, place them full-time at nonprofits for a year, and pay each an $85,000 salary plus benefits. The company committed an initial $150M to the program, which it runs with CodePath and Social Finance, framing it as direct investment in workers absorbing AI-driven economic disruption.

title: Phoenix LiveView 1.2
blurb: Phoenix LiveView 1.2 adds colocated CSS, letting you write scoped styles inside HEEx templates using the modern @scope rule.

title: From Third-Party to First-Party: Measuring and Protecting Against Modern Web Tracking Mechanisms
blurb: A new study finds that over 54% of top websites now use first-party or server-side tracking, relocating tracking logic into the browser's first-party context or the site's backend to evade traditional client-side detection tools. The authors introduce a provider-independent method to detect these mechanisms and map the ecosystem by clustering similar scripts into a network graph.

title: MIPSBLEED: Uncovering Microarchitectural Timing Leaks in Pervasive Embedded Processors
blurb: MIPSBLEED is an exploitation framework showing that simultaneous multithreading on MIPS processors opens cross-core timing channels in the L1 data cache, L1 instruction cache, and execution engine. Using unprivileged assembly probes, the authors recover an elliptic-curve key in a single trace, flagging the routers, industrial controllers, and IoT devices built on MIPS as an overlooked side-channel target.

title: Council of Europe investigates ShinyHunters data breach claims
blurb: The Council of Europe, the continent's oldest intergovernmental body, is investigating ShinyHunters' claim to have stolen more than 429,000 HR and payroll documents, including 409,000-plus payslips covering 10,000 staff from 2011 to 2026. The extortion group, recently tied to Salesforce, Snowflake, and Oracle PeopleSoft breaches affecting hundreds of organizations, threatened to leak the files containing names, bank details, and medical records.

title: Binance Prepares for EU Exit With Local License in Limbo
blurb: Binance is preparing to halt its services across the European Union as a deadline to secure a bloc-wide operating license approaches without approval. The crypto exchange has yet to win clearance from Greece's financial regulator, leaving its local license in limbo and threatening its access to one of the world's largest regulated markets.

title: Attackers hijacked over 1,500 Arch Linux packages to steal developers’ secrets, no hacking required
blurb: Attackers seized more than 1,500 packages in the Arch User Repository and rewrote their build scripts to install a credential stealer, without exploiting any software flaw. They adopted orphaned packages to inherit established trust, then harvested SSH keys, GitHub and npm tokens, and Slack and Vault credentials from the developers whose machines hold the keys to the next supply-chain attack.

title: Anthropic locks enterprises out of Fable and Mythos following government order
blurb: Anthropic disabled Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all customers after a US government export control directive barred foreign nationals, including Anthropic's own employees, from using its most powerful models. The Friday order cited national security concerns over a reported jailbreak that Anthropic says exposed only minor, already-known vulnerabilities, leaving enterprise CIOs suddenly cut off from frontier capabilities by government action rather than vendor choice.

title: Salesforce buys Fin for $3.6bn
blurb: Salesforce agreed to acquire Fin, the customer-service AI agent formerly known as Intercom, for $3.6B, with the deal expected to close in Q4 pending regulatory clearance. Fin's multichannel agent, which claims a 76% resolution rate across 12,000 customers, will fold into Salesforce's Agentforce platform, which Benioff says now runs at a $1.2B annual rate, up 205% year over year.

title: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
blurb: OpenAI's net loss attributable to the company reached $38.53 billion in 2025, nearly 8 times its 2024 loss, on $13.07 billion in revenue against $34 billion in costs and expenses, according to audited financials verified by the Financial Times. The jump was driven partly by the company's conversion from a nonprofit to a for-profit, which added a $41.55 billion charge tied to changes in fair value of convertible interests and warrant liabilities.

title: Context-Aware RL for Agentic and Multimodal LLMs
blurb: ContextRL trains LLMs to ground answers in decisive evidence by rewarding selection of the supporting context, gaining +2.2% over standard GRPO.

title: Speaking the Language of Science: Toward a General-Purpose Generative Foundation Model for the Natural Sciences
blurb: LOGOS (Language Of Generative Objects in Science) is a generative language model that unifies tasks across the natural sciences in a single autoregressive framework built on a shared scientific grammar. It encodes scientific objects and their spatial interactions as token sequences over a common vocabulary, representing contact and constraint patterns as discrete tokens so that structural interactions are captured without explicit coordinates or geometric neural networks, and matches or outperforms domain-specific baselines across diverse tasks.

title: Copilot Cowork is now generally available
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available worldwide after a three-month Frontier preview, during which more than half of the Fortune 500 adopted it, including Accenture, Capital Group, Koch, and Zurich Insurance. Cowork runs complex, long-running, multi-tool tasks end-to-end in the cloud, and the GA release adds model choice, plugin extensibility, and usage-based billing with new cost controls.

title: Snap unveils $2,195 Specs, its first AR glasses that are "fully standalone" and have a 51-degree field of view, expected "this fall" in the US, UK, and France (Jay Peters/The Verge)
blurb: Snap announced Specs, its first standalone AR glasses, priced at $2,195 with a 51-degree field of view and no required tethered phone or puck. The company expects to ship the glasses this fall in the US, UK, and France, marking its most serious push yet to compete with Meta and other AR headset makers.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to its most powerful models after the company pulled its Fable and Mythos systems. Anthropic said both sides are working quickly to resolve the dispute, marking its first public comment since the removal and signaling the rising stakes of government oversight over frontier AI capabilities.

title: Endpoint Security Startup Ent Emerges From Stealth With $100 Million Seed Round
blurb: Ent emerged from stealth with $100M in seed funding, led by Decibel, to build intent-aware endpoint security that interprets user and AI agent behavior before risky actions complete. Founded by RiskIQ veterans Elias Manousos and Brandon Dixon, the company deploys a lightweight agent that runs real-time AI reasoning on the device, extending existing EDR, SIEM, SOAR, and IAM tools rather than replacing them.

title: Arch Linux locks down AUR signups amid wave of malicious commits
blurb: Arch Linux paused new AUR signups after a wave of malicious commits, requiring manual approval for accounts uploading user-submitted packages.

title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
blurb: Anthropic suspended access to its latest model, Fable 5, three days after launch and restricted its Mythos 5 models, complying with a US national-security order barring foreign nationals, including its own employees, from using the technology. The directive followed Anthropic research showing adversaries increasingly use frontier models to write malicious code, find vulnerabilities, and automate attack chains, with UK government testing confirming the models can run end-to-end intrusions.

title: Android 17 arrives on Pixel phones today
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to compatible Pixel phones today as part of the June Pixel Drop, with other manufacturers expected to ship the update throughout 2026. The release adds Bubbles floating app windows, Screen Reactions for reaction videos, and foldable gaming controls, plus Pixel-exclusive features like the Gemini Omni text-to-video model and expanded Quick Share AirDrop support.

title: Mobileye puts self-driving tech in 230 million cars. Now it wants to run its own robotaxis.
blurb: Mobileye announced plans to launch its own robotaxi service in a major US city in 2027, moving from supplying self-driving tech for over 230 million cars to operating an autonomous ride-hailing business itself. The shift puts Mobileye in direct competition with carmakers that license its Mobileye Drive system, starting with roughly 100 driverless vehicles and aiming to scale to 17,000 within five years.

title: ROVE: Unlocking Human Interventions for Humanoid Manipulation via Reinforcement Learning
blurb: ROVE is a reinforcement learning framework that post-trains humanoid Vision-Language-Action models using imperfect human corrections, filtering out the hesitant or erroneous moves that typically degrade intervention-based training. The system pairs a human-in-the-loop data pipeline with Optimistic Value Estimation to prioritize high-value behaviors from mixed-quality trajectories, and folds in cross-embodiment human videos to make value estimates more robust.

title: MyPCBench: A Benchmark for Personally Intelligent Computer-Use Agents
blurb: MyPCBench tests computer-use agents as personal assistants on a simulated Linux desktop with 184 tasks; Claude Opus 4.6 leads, fully solving 55.4%.

title: Threads adds new personalization and community features as it reaches 500M monthly users
blurb: Threads reached 500M monthly active users and rolled out "Your Algo," a feature that lets users privately tell the feed to show more or less of specific topics for one, three, or seven days. Meta's platform also moved Communities out of beta with a new Communities Hub for discovery, distinct community icons, and expanded Live Chats, positioning Threads against X with controls rivals don't offer.

title: Apple releasing 20th anniversary iPhone, AirPods with cameras next year: report
blurb: Apple is preparing a 20th-anniversary iPhone with a nearly edge-to-edge curved display, a second-generation foldable, and camera-equipped AirPods for late 2027, according to a Bloomberg report from Mark Gurman. The anniversary models and foldable will run a 2-nanometer A21 chip, while the AirPods, delayed from 2026 by Apple's AI and Siri struggles, will use visual models to identify objects around the user.

title: Anthropic’s new privacy policy offers US consumers a way around the Fable ban
blurb: Anthropic updated its privacy policy to let it collect government IDs, biometric data, and facial geometry to verify a user's age or identity, taking effect July 8. The change gives the company a way to restore consumer access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, which a US export order forced it to disable for everyone because it could not distinguish foreign nationals from US citizens.

title: Dynamic Malicious Skills in Agentic AI
blurb: This paper demonstrates a new attack on agentic AI in which malicious instructions hidden in a skill's natural-language documentation trick an agent into injecting harmful logic into an otherwise benign skill at runtime. The authors confirm the attack works against frameworks like OpenHands and Claude Code, and propose a defense using OS kernel-enforced read-only mounts that blocks dynamic skill modification while preserving normal functionality.

title: Salesforce to buy AI customer service platform Fin for $3.6 billion to boost agentic offerings
blurb: Salesforce will buy AI customer service platform Fin, formerly Intercom, for about $3.6B to expand its agentic AI lineup. The deal complements Agentforce by adding Fin's AI agent, which resolves chat, email, WhatsApp, text, phone, and Slack queries, and lands as Salesforce fends off fears that AI tools could make its SaaS model obsolete amid a stock down over a third in 2026.

title: Orchestrated Reality: From Role-Play to Living, Playable Game Worlds -- LLM-Driven World Simulation as a Parameterized-Action POMDP
blurb: This paper proposes "orchestrated reality," a framework that treats an LLM-driven game world as a canonical object owned by a single orchestration agent modeled on a tabletop RPG Game Master. The authors formalize the system as a Parameterized-Action POMDP where state is a tree of schema-validated JSON entities and a Plan-Diff-Validate-Apply pipeline commits content-hashed deltas, aiming to fix the problem of LLMs asserting world state in free prose without a validated representation.

title: Robinhood cuts 10% of staff, expects $28 million in restructuring charges
blurb: Robinhood cut 10% of its staff and expects roughly $28 million in restructuring charges, tied to a push to flatten management layers and improve efficiency. The reductions come as the company trims headcount across the organization rather than a single division, signaling a broader effort to cut costs and speed up decision-making.

title: Mobileye is entering the US robotaxi market with standalone service
blurb: Mobileye will launch a vertically integrated US robotaxi service in an unnamed city in 2027, using its Moovit platform to handle bookings and fleet coordination. The Israeli firm, spun out of Intel in 2022, plans to start with around 100 vehicles early next year as it shifts from supplying autonomous-driving tech to owning the operation.

title: Qualcomm mulls taking over Jim Keller's Tenstorrent, report claims — deal for AI chipmaker would value the company at between $8 billion and $10 billion
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to acquire Jim Keller's AI chipmaker Tenstorrent at a valuation between $8B and $10B, according to The Information. The deal would be one of Qualcomm's largest ever and is driven by interest in Tenstorrent's RISC-V AI accelerators and data center CPU IP, though it would add to an already crowded lineup that includes the company's Hexagon-based AI200 and AI250 accelerators and recently acquired Ventana Micro.

title: LLM-as-Code Agentic Programming for Agent Harness
blurb: This paper argues that LLM agent failures like token explosion, control-flow hallucination, and unreliable completion are architectural, not bugs, stemming from making a probabilistic model orchestrate deterministic looping and branching. The authors propose Agentic Programming, where the program governs all control flow and the LLM becomes an embedded "LLM-as-Code" component called only for reasoning or generation, with each call's context built from the execution history's call tree as a DAG.

title: Compositional Reasoning Depth Predicts Clinical AI Failure: Empirical Evidence Consistent with Transformer Compositionality Limits in Electronic Health Record Question Answering
blurb: Across three models from two providers, EHR question-answering accuracy declines monotonically as required reasoning steps increase, predicting clinical AI failure.

title: Bing Webmaster Tools updates AI reporting with Intents, Topics, Citation Share and Compare
blurb: Bing Webmaster Tools added Intents, Topics, Citation Share, and Compare to its AI performance report, now rolling out in global preview.

title: Google launches 'bubble' multi-tasking and foldable gaming mode with Android 17
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to Pixel devices today, adding Bubbles for floating multi-app windows, a 50/50 foldable gaming mode, and granular location and contact permissions. Gemini Intelligence, Google's context-aware system for multi-step tasks, arrives on select advanced devices this summer, while the OS reaches other Android phones throughout the year.

title: ChatGPT’s market share slips below 50% for first time
blurb: ChatGPT's share of the AI assistant market dropped below 50% for the first time, even as it stayed the most-used chatbot worldwide with over 1.1 billion monthly users. Gemini and Claude trail at 662 million and 245 million monthly users respectively, signaling that rivals are closing the gap as OpenAI's early lead erodes.

title: Databricks declares the end of pipelines with a unified platform for operational and analytical data
blurb: Databricks introduced Lake Transactional/Analytical Processing (LTAP) at its Data + AI Summit, a Lakebase-built architecture that runs operational and analytical workloads on a single copy of data in the lake, eliminating the CDC and ETL pipelines that traditionally sync the two. The company also released a real-time analytics engine that it says delivers millisecond responses without separate serving infrastructure, pitching the unified stack as better suited to AI agents that read and act on data continuously.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: The curl project will pause all vulnerability report handling during July 2026 to give its maintainers a rest after months of submission pressure.

title: France's digital sovereignty push is struggling to escape the Microsoft gravity well
blurb: France's drive for digital sovereignty remains heavily dependent on Microsoft, with government agencies and public institutions still routing core systems through the company's software and cloud services. The report details how procurement inertia, entrenched integrations, and a lack of mature local alternatives keep European-built options on the margins, undercutting the political goal of reducing reliance on US tech providers.

title: What the Anthropic shutdown means for Europe
blurb: Anthropic suspended access to its most advanced AI models after the US government ordered it to cut off foreign nationals, then pulled access for all customers, including American ones, to stay compliant. The episode marks the first activation of a US digital "kill switch" over technology that European organizations rely on for critical services, and has intensified European calls for sovereign AI infrastructure.

title: Google launches Wear OS 7 with Live Updates and a battery life boost
blurb: Google began rolling out Wear OS 7 to the Pixel Watch 2, 3, and 4, adding Live Updates that sync ongoing events like sports scores and delivery tracking between watch and phone. The update claims up to 10% more battery life than Wear OS 6, with Gemini Intelligence features such as natural-language widget creation and multi-step app automation arriving later this year.

title: Salesforce to acquire customer service automation startup Fin for $3.6B
blurb: Salesforce announced plans to acquire Fin, the customer service software provider formerly known as Intercom, for about $3.6B. The deal folds Fin's automation tools into Salesforce's Agentforce AI suite, deepening its push into autonomous customer support as it competes for enterprise AI deployments.

title: Databricks agrees to acquire Panther Labs, its third cybersecurity acquisition; Panther was valued at $1.4B when it raised a $120M Series B in 2021 (Jeffrey Dastin/Reuters)
blurb: Databricks agreed to acquire Panther Labs, a security information and event management vendor, in its third cybersecurity acquisition. Panther was valued at $1.4B when it raised a $120M Series B in 2021, and the deal extends Databricks' push to fold security analytics into its data and AI platform.

title: Anthropic to meet with Trump administration over Mythos dispute
blurb: Anthropic will meet with Trump administration officials in Washington on Monday to resolve a federal directive that forced it to suspend access to its new Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The Friday order cited an unspecified national security threat and barred use by any foreign national, deepening a rift that already saw the Pentagon label Anthropic a supply chain risk in March.

title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
blurb: The Justice Department asked a federal court to halt a citizen pollution lawsuit against xAI's data center in Memphis, citing national security and Elon Musk's role in the recent Iran war. The DOJ also argued it has the authority to block environmental suits brought by citizens, a claim that could reshape how communities challenge industrial facilities under federal law.

title: Chrome is removing the last workaround keeping Manifest V2 ad blockers alive
blurb: Google will remove the last workaround keeping Manifest V2 ad blockers running in Chrome, with Chrome 150 deleting the developer flag on June 30 and Chrome 151 clearing the remaining references in July. Engineers cited Manifest V2's security liabilities and maintenance burden, leaving users on uBlock Origin to switch to the weaker MV3-compatible Lite version or move to Firefox or Brave.

title: SpaceX leapfrogs Amazon in market cap, briefly jumps Microsoft among top U.S. companies
blurb: SpaceX briefly overtook Microsoft on Tuesday to become the fourth-largest U.S. company, with its market cap touching $2.94 trillion after shares popped 9% and leapfrogged Amazon. The surge comes days after a record IPO and the same day SpaceX announced a $60B acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor, though CFRA initiated coverage with a sell rating, citing the company's $4.9B 2025 net loss and elevated valuation.

title: Leaked financial docs show OpenAI is losing billions of dollars a year
blurb: Leaked audited financials obtained by journalist Ed Zitron show OpenAI's revenue grew from $3.7B in 2024 to $13.07B in 2025, while its loss from operations widened from $8.78B to $20.92B over the same period. R&D alone hit $19.18B in 2025, including $10.59B paid to Microsoft, complicating the company's pitch to investors that it can reach profitability by 2030 ahead of an expected IPO.

title: New expanded Android parental controls to support families around the world
blurb: Google is expanding its Android Parental Controls to all devices updating to Android 17, bringing screen-time limits, downtime schedules, Google Play content filters, and per-app blocking into a single PIN-protected settings hub previously available only on Pixel. The company also raised its U.S. digital wellbeing fund to more than $50M to back interventions on healthier technology use and combating social isolation among kids and teens.

title: Caddy compatibility for zeroserve: 3x throughput and 70% lower latency
blurb: zeroserve added a Caddy-compatibility mode that JIT-compiles a Caddyfile to eBPF and then to native x86_64/ARM64 machine code, running it in an io_uring event loop. As an HTTPS reverse proxy on two threads, the approach delivered 3x higher throughput and 70% lower latency than Caddy, while still supporting Turing-complete eBPF middleware that can be called directly from the Caddyfile.

title: Bland, which uses proprietary, in-house-built voice models to process calls for 250+ enterprise clients, raised a $50M Series C led by Dell Technologies Capital (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Bland raised a $50M Series C led by Dell Technologies Capital to expand its enterprise voice platform. The startup uses proprietary, in-house-built voice models to handle calls for more than 250 enterprise clients, positioning it against AI voice rivals that lean on third-party speech models.

title: Google says LLMS.txt files won’t harm or help your search rankings
blurb: Google updated its AI Search optimization guide to confirm that LLMS.txt files neither help nor hurt rankings, because Google Search ignores them entirely. The clarification settles months of SEO confusion over AI-readable files: Google may crawl and index them like any file, but gives them no special treatment, so they only matter for other services that actually consume them.

title: AMD takes over MEXT for memory tiering tech that enables flash to appear as DRAM to applications — tech to 'address growing memory constraints' in the data center
blurb: AMD acquired MEXT, a startup whose AI-based memory tiering makes NAND flash appear as DRAM to applications, moving infrequently accessed data to cheaper storage while predicting which pages to pull back before they're needed. AMD plans to fold MEXT's Predictive Memory Engine into its data center portfolio to cut DRAM costs and ease the memory bottlenecks increasingly throttling large AI workloads.

title: Configuration Smells in AGENTS.md Files: Common Mistakes in Configuring Coding Agents
blurb: The first catalog of "configuration smells" in AGENTS.md files finds them widespread across 100 popular repos, with Lint Leakage affecting 62%.

title: Even more batteries included with Emacs
blurb: The third installment of a series surfacing useful, lesser-known features built into stock Emacs, like dictionary-tooltip-mode.

title: Fox’s $22B Roku acquisition aims to expand its reach into smart TVs, advertising
blurb: Fox Corporation agreed to acquire Roku for $160 per share, valuing the streaming company at roughly $22 billion and combining Fox's broadcast and Tubi businesses with Roku's 100-million-household platform. The deal centers on Roku OS and its advertising arm, which pulled in $371 million in ad revenue last quarter, and would create the third-largest US player by share of TV viewing.

title: Fox Buys Roku, The Problem With Fox’s Smart Strategy, Streaming That Works
blurb: Fox acquired Roku, betting that owning a streaming distribution platform is worth more than extracting maximum fees from rights holders. The market sold off on the deal, but the analysis argues Fox is trading its position as a content extractor for leverage as a "renter" that controls the pipe to viewers, a bet that streaming distribution now matters more than content negotiation.

title: Microsoft faces down sueball, capacity problems in series of challenges
blurb: Microsoft is contending with a shareholder lawsuit alleging it made misleading statements about Copilot and AI adoption, alongside mounting datacenter capacity constraints. The combination highlights the gap between Microsoft's aggressive AI marketing and the infrastructure and revenue reality, as investors press for proof that Copilot demand justifies the company's spending.

title: DocLang aims to make documents readable by AI, not humans
blurb: IBM, Nvidia, and Red Hat have formed a working group, hosted by the Linux Foundation's LF AI & Data project, to build DocLang, an open document format designed for AI systems rather than human readers. The spec defines a structured, machine-readable format built for LLM tokenizers, extending the DocLing toolkit so enterprises can prepare and govern document data more reliably than they can with PDFs and other human-oriented formats.

title: Letter: Hightouch, an ad tech startup recently valued at $2.75B, offers to buy LiveRamp's identity business from Publicis for $800M to $1.2B in cash and stock (Sara Fischer/Axios)
blurb: Hightouch, an ad tech startup recently valued at $2.75B, offered to buy LiveRamp's identity business from Publicis for $800M to $1.2B in cash and stock, according to a letter reported by Axios. The bid targets LiveRamp's identity-resolution assets, a core piece of the infrastructure advertisers use to match users across platforms in a post-cookie market.

title: Microsoft is moving Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and is considering a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a cheaper option (Ina Fried/Axios)
blurb: Microsoft is shifting Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and weighing a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a lower-cost model option, according to Axios. The moves point to mounting pressure on Copilot's economics, as Microsoft looks to control inference costs while giving enterprise customers cheaper alternatives to frontier OpenAI models.

title: Mobileye’s US robotaxi launch will put it on both sides of the AV business
blurb: Mobileye said it will launch a robotaxi service in an unnamed US city in 2027, starting with 100 autonomous vehicles and aiming to scale to roughly 17,000 within five years. The move makes the Intel subsidiary both an AV technology supplier and an operator, putting it in direct competition with customers like Volkswagen that already license its self-driving system.

title: SpaceX stock jumps 20% in first full day of trading after record debut
blurb: SpaceX stock rose 20% on its first full trading day, closing at $192.50 after the largest IPO in history valued the company above $2 trillion. The rally has sparked debate over the valuation, with CFRA initiating a sell rating at a $115 target and Morningstar pegging fair value at $63, against 2025 revenue of $18.7 billion and a near-$5 billion loss.

title: DeepSeek Becomes China’s Most Valuable AI Startup After $7.4 Billion Fundraise
blurb: DeepSeek raised more than $7.4B in its first external funding round, making it China's most valuable AI startup. The capital backs the costly development of its frontier models, which gained global attention for matching Western labs at a fraction of the reported training spend.

title: Self-driving tech supplier Mobileye targets U.S. robotaxi launch in 2027
blurb: Mobileye said it will launch its own U.S. robotaxi service in 2027, deploying an initial 100-vehicle fleet in a major city and scaling to roughly 17,000 vehicles over five years. The move puts the Jerusalem-based supplier in direct competition with automakers that use its Mobileye Drive technology, entering a market led by Waymo, which now operates across 11 U.S. cities.

title: AI note-taking is now available in Google Voice
blurb: Google Voice now takes AI notes and transcribes calls, accessible only to whoever started the capture and gated behind admin consent settings.

title: Dan Ives on US-Anthropic Talks, AI M&A, Open AI Losses
blurb: Wedbush analyst Dan Ives expects AI dealmaking to accelerate, citing a "tug of war" between Anthropic and the Trump administration and mounting losses at OpenAI ahead of its planned IPO later this year. Ives frames the regulatory friction and consolidation pressure as defining forces for the sector, with OpenAI's reported losses raising questions about valuations heading into a major public offering.

title: SpaceX Is Now More Valuable Than Amazon, Meta and Many Others
blurb: SpaceX vaulted into the world's top five most valuable companies in its first few days of public trading, surpassing Amazon and Meta. Elon Musk's rocket maker now ranks among the largest firms globally by market capitalization, a debut that underscores investor appetite for its launch business and Starlink satellite network.

title: Rare-Earth Startup Lines Up $500 Million in Pentagon Funding
blurb: Phoenix Tailings secured a conditional $500M loan from the Pentagon to expand its US rare-earth refining capacity. The deal is the latest White House push to build domestic supply of rare-earth elements and reduce dependence on China, which dominates global processing.

title: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first debt sale since start of AI boom
blurb: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first bond sale since the AI boom began, with sources saying the total could approach $25 billion. The move follows similar capital raises from Alphabet, Amazon, and Super Micro, and comes as Nvidia funds an $80 billion buyback program after generating $49 billion in free cash flow last quarter.

title: Databricks Releases General AI Agents for Businesses
blurb: Databricks released general-purpose AI agents that handle open-ended business work like research, analysis, and report generation across a company's data. The launch pushes the data-analytics firm beyond its infrastructure roots into the AI applications layer, where it competes with OpenAI, Microsoft, and a crowded field of enterprise agent startups.

title: Google develops Personalization controls for Gemini
blurb: Google is building hidden Gemini controls that prioritize paid subscriptions and let users inspect and edit context on a per-app basis.

title: Light-driven memory breakthrough targets AI power consumption
blurb: Researchers at Japan's QST demonstrated a magnetic memory material that rewrites with ultrashort laser pulses instead of electric current, using a CoFeB artificial ferrimagnet switched by a single femtosecond pulse. Built on a material already common in spintronic memory and compatible with magnetic tunnel junctions, the approach could cut the heat and energy losses of electrically driven memory while enabling faster write speeds for AI data centers.

title: Anthropic Dispatches Staff to D.C., Racing to Resolve AI Export Restrictions
blurb: Anthropic has dispatched staff to Washington, D.C. to negotiate an end to export restrictions that forced it to shut down its most powerful AI models in certain markets. The company is seeking a deal with the government to resolve the controls, underscoring how policy decisions, not just model quality, now shape where frontier AI can operate.

title: Anthropic suspends Fable and Mythos systems "for all users" after US government claims jailbreak risk
blurb: Anthropic suspended its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all users days after release, complying with Department of Commerce export controls imposed by the Trump administration over an alleged jailbreak vulnerability. The company disputed the reasoning, arguing the "narrow, non-universal jailbreak" exists in rival models like OpenAI's GPT-5.5 and that the standard, if applied industry-wide, would halt all frontier deployments.

title: Exclusive: Mindbeam touts dramatic performance improvements in CPU-based AI inference
blurb: Mindbeam released Litespark-Inference, an open-source framework that runs ternary LLMs on standard Apple, Intel, AMD, and Arm CPUs with 17x to 96x higher throughput and over 80% lower memory use than standard PyTorch. The approach constrains model weights to -1, 0, and +1 to cut multiplication overhead, positioning CPUs as complements to scarce GPUs for cost-sensitive, memory-constrained edge inference.

title: SpaceX Set to Overtake Microsoft, Amazon in Value as Stock Soars
blurb: SpaceX shares surged Tuesday, putting the company on track to pass Amazon and Microsoft and become the fourth-largest publicly traded firm in the world just days after its debut. The rally marks a more than 50% jump across three sessions, a rapid repricing that ranks the rocket maker alongside the biggest US tech companies within its first week on the market.

title: Hetzner Price Adjustment
blurb: Hetzner raised prices on new dedicated and cloud server orders and rescales starting June 15, 2026.

title: SpaceX IPO raises total of $85.7 billion as underwriters exercise 'greenshoe' overallotment option
blurb: SpaceX raised a total of $85.7 billion in its IPO after underwriters exercised the greenshoe overallotment option, adding to the initial $75 billion raised Thursday. The sale makes Elon Musk's space and AI company one of the largest public offerings on record, giving it deep capital to fund Starship development and its growing AI ambitions.

title: DOJ claims xAI’s unpermitted gas turbines are a matter of ‘national, economic, and energy security’
blurb: The DOJ sided with xAI in a lawsuit brought by the NAACP that seeks to shut down dozens of unpermitted natural gas turbines powering Elon Musk's data centers near Memphis. The Justice Department argued that Grok supports "mission-critical" military operations, so cutting the power would threaten national, economic, and energy security, even as the SpaceX division plans to buy another $2.8B in turbines over three years.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: Ahrefs analyzed 137K domains and found 97% of published llms.txt files got zero traffic in May 2026, mostly bots when fetched.

title: UK Regulator Weighs Cutting Data Center Power Use at Peak Times
blurb: Britain's energy regulator Ofgem is weighing rules that would require future data centers to cut power consumption during periods of grid stress. The proposal is part of a broader effort to absorb surging electricity demand from AI and cloud computing without overloading the network or stalling new construction.

title: Plaud says its software business topped $100M in ARR after shipping over 2M AI notetakers
blurb: Plaud said its software business passed $100M in annualized revenue run rate after selling more than 2 million of its screen-free AI notetakers, including the $179 Plaud Pin S. The subscription revenue rides entirely on hardware owners, with nearly half of device users upgrading from the free tier to paid plans, a rare proof point for a consumer AI hardware company.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Limits on Its $200-a-Month AI Plans
blurb: A proposed class-action lawsuit accuses Anthropic of overselling usage allowances on its $200-a-month Claude subscription tiers, alleging customers paid for capacity the company did not deliver. The suit, which is seeking class-action status, centers on limits Anthropic placed on its priciest plans, adding legal pressure as AI providers tighten usage caps to manage soaring inference costs.

title: US battery manufacturing output continues to break records
blurb: US battery manufacturing output hit another record high in early 2026, according to Federal Reserve industrial production data tracking the sector. The sustained climb reflects a wave of domestic gigafactory buildouts driven by EV demand and IRA manufacturing incentives, marking a steep departure from years of flat output before 2021.

title: Xreal unveils Aura, its lightweight smart glasses powered by Android XR
blurb: Xreal released Aura, lightweight see-through XR glasses running Google's Android XR and Qualcomm's new Snapdragon Reality Elite chip, launching this Fall. Weighing under 95 grams, Aura offers a 70-degree field of view, six-degrees-of-freedom hand tracking, and deep Gemini integration, with access to Google Play apps that float as windows or anchor to real-world surfaces.

title: Salesforce acquires Fin, formerly known as Intercom
blurb: Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire Fin, the customer-support agent vendor that rebranded from Intercom a month ago, for roughly $3.6B. The deal is Salesforce's fifth acquisition of 2026 and folds Fin's Apex support model into Agentforce, which hit $1.2B in ARR last quarter, up 20% year over year.

title: Tianwen-2 makes series of burns on approach to asteroid, according to radio tracking
blurb: China's Tianwen-2 spacecraft completed a series of small propulsive burns after its June 7 main maneuver, setting up a July rendezvous with near-Earth asteroid 469219 Kamoʻoalewa. The probe will map the 40-100-meter quasi-moon and collect samples using three redundant techniques, then return them to Earth before flying on to main-belt comet 311P, arriving in 2035.

title: Qualcomm unveils its Snapdragon Reality Elite chip for next-gen AR headsets
blurb: Qualcomm released the Snapdragon Reality Elite, a new top-tier XR chip that supports 4.4K-per-eye resolution at 90 fps while running 12C cooler and extending battery life up to 20% over the XR2+ Gen 2. It delivers 60% higher GPU, 30% higher CPU, and 160% higher NPU performance, enabling smaller see-through headsets with on-device AI features like photorealistic avatars, starting with Xreal's Aura glasses.

title: Boot Naked Linux
blurb: A developer strips a Linux system down to just the kernel and a custom C init to achieve near-instant boot times.

title: ChapsVision to replace Palantir in major contract with French intelligence agency
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency DGSI ended its decade-long contract with Palantir, replacing the US firm with French startup ChapsVision to analyze its mass datasets. The switch follows Germany's intelligence agency making the same move last month and Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu's push to "build real autonomy," reflecting growing European fears of a US "digital kill switch" over critical AI and cloud systems.

title: HPE Boosts Networking Gear for AI Clients, Adds Siemens Energy
blurb: HPE introduced new networking gear for AI data centers, extending the technology it gained from its Juniper Networks acquisition to win more corporate AI workloads. The company also added Siemens Energy as a customer, signaling demand for higher-performance fabric as enterprises scale up AI deployments beyond pilots.

title: Payments startup Flutterwave hits $3.2B valuation, backed by Ripple
blurb: Flutterwave raised a Series E that values the African payments company at $3.2B, with Ripple taking an equity stake to jointly expand financial services across the continent. The undisclosed round pushes Flutterwave's total funding past $500M and gives it Ripple's infrastructure to grow digital asset offerings across the 35 African countries where it operates.

title: Microsoft’s Latest Surface PCs Offer Better Graphics, Faster AI
blurb: Microsoft introduced refreshed Surface Pro and Surface Laptop models with faster graphics and new Qualcomm chips aimed at creative professionals and AI power users. The update leans on improved on-device AI performance to differentiate the lineup, as Microsoft pushes Windows hardware toward local AI workloads rather than relying solely on the cloud.

title: Wear OS 7 arrives to Pixel watches with Gemini AI and live updates
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7 for the Pixel Watch 2, 3, and 4, adding Gemini-powered widget creation, Live Updates for game scores and deliveries, and control over earbuds and upcoming Android XR smartglasses. The update also delivers roughly 10% better battery life, though Google has not said when it will reach Samsung Galaxy Watches or other Wear OS devices.

title: Honda Civics and Installing Software With Android Test Keys
blurb: Researcher found 2012-era Android infotainment in a 2021 Honda Civic accepts USB updates signed with leftover AOSP test keys, enabling arbitrary code execution.

title: Microsoft announces 12th-gen Surface Pro with Snapdragon X2 processors
blurb: Microsoft released the 12th-gen Surface Pro, swapping the Snapdragon X1 for Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2 chips that deliver up to 53% faster graphics, an 80 TOPS NPU, and 15.5 hours of battery life. The base configuration now starts at $1,499, up from $999 last generation, with RAM options extending to 64GB and a free keyboard promo running through June 30.

title: Google Open-Sources a Knowledge Format and Wires It Into Its Catalog
blurb: Google Cloud published the Open Knowledge Format on June 12, a 451-line spec that represents AI-agent knowledge as a directory of plain markdown files requiring just one field, and the same day updated its Knowledge Catalog to ingest and serve that format to agents. By open-sourcing the portable format while keeping storage, serving, and access control in its paid catalog, Google commoditizes the knowledge layer and routes demand toward the products it still sells.

title: Researchers recycle old phones and cluster them into ‘computing platforms’ that operate as a low-cost data center — says processors on modern smartphones deliver higher single-core performance than comparable multicore servers
blurb: UCSD researchers, working with Google, stripped retired Pixel phones down to their motherboards, replaced Android with a data-center Linux distro, and clustered them into a low-cost computing platform managed by Kubernetes. The team found that 25 to 50 three-year-old phones match a dual-socket server CPU on per-core performance, and plans to scale to 2,000 phones to cut both e-waste and data-center demand.

title: Game Engine White Papers: Commander Keen
blurb: The Game Engine White Papers: Commander Keen is a 214-page, full-color book detailing how the 1990 platformer was built on late-eighties hardware, covering the 80286 CPU, EGA video cards, sound cards, game assets, and the engine itself. It is available as a paid paperback on Amazon or a free high-res PDF, with the book's source code published on GitHub.

title: Malaysia’s AI agent-powered messaging app Respond.io raises $62.5M, eyes acquisitions
blurb: Respond.io, a Kuala Lumpur-based customer messaging platform that uses AI agents to handle inquiries and close sales across WhatsApp, Instagram, and other channels, raised a $62.5M Series B led by Camber Partners. The company reports $35M in ARR growing 169% year-over-year at a 30% profit margin, and plans to pursue acquisitions in North America and Europe.

title: French Security Service to Replace Palantir With Local Firm
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency DGSI will drop Palantir's data tools in favor of software from local firm Chapsvision. The switch reflects a broader European push to cut reliance on US tech providers for sensitive government work, giving homegrown vendors like Chapsvision a foothold in markets Palantir has dominated.

title: RiskIQ founders launch Ent Security with $100M to rethink endpoint defense
blurb: Ent Security launched with $100M to build intent-aware endpoint defense that evaluates what users and AI agents are doing and intervenes before risky actions complete. Founded by RiskIQ veterans Elias Manousos and Brandon Dixon, who sold that company to Microsoft in 2021, Ent runs a lightweight on-device agent and is already deployed across Global 2000 firms in hospitality, financial services, and defense.

title: Carvana is expanding into new vehicles. The implications could reshape the U.S. automotive retail market
blurb: Carvana has quietly bought seven new-vehicle franchises since last year, mostly selling Stellantis' Chrysler, Dodge, Jeep, and Ram brands, marking its first move beyond used cars. Its Casa Grande, Arizona store sold over 700 new vehicles last month to become Stellantis' top-volume location nationally, a jump from 30 to 50 monthly before the acquisition that analysts say could disrupt the $1.3T franchised dealer system.

title: Microsoft’s new Surface Laptop has a trackpad that rumbles like a game controller
blurb: Microsoft's new Surface Laptop and Surface Pro add a haptic trackpad that vibrates during actions like window snapping, PowerPoint alignment, and video scrubbing, with the same feedback extended to the Slim Pen. Both consumer models run exclusively on Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2, with no Intel option outside the business line, and start at $1,600 and $1,500 respectively.

title: Microsoft unveils new Surface Laptop with improved trackpad, Snapdragon X2, and more
blurb: Microsoft announced the eighth-gen Surface Laptop with Snapdragon X2 chips, a haptic trackpad, 80 TOPS NPUs, and a $1,599 starting price.

title: Radical Numerics launches with $50M to build general biological intelligence
blurb: Radical Numerics launched with $50M to build "general biological intelligence," multimodal models that learn directly from DNA, RNA, and proteins to fold biology's separate strands into one system. Founded by the team behind Evo, the first AI model to read and write DNA at scale, the lab previewed Omnii, a genomic model it says sets state of the art on causal regulatory variants and detecting AI-engineered pathogens.

title: Rio de Janeiro's city government model Rio3.5 beats Qwen3.7 in recent benchmarks
blurb: Rio de Janeiro's municipal IT company released Rio3.5, a 397B-parameter model that reportedly beats Alibaba's Qwen3.7 on recent benchmarks.

title: China's supreme court bans Infineon from selling GaN power chips in China — market-leader Innoscience secures major victory in multi-region patent war
blurb: China's Supreme People's Court upheld an injunction barring Infineon from selling the disputed gallium nitride power chips in mainland China, the final ruling in a patent case brought by market leader Innoscience. Both companies sit on Nvidia's approved supplier list for 800V AI-rack power delivery, and the multi-region war has split decisions across courts, with a separate US ITC ruling and German cases favoring Infineon.

title: NASA said nyet to Roscosmos plan to cut into leaky ISS segment
blurb: NASA rejected a Roscosmos proposal to cut into the leak-prone PrK transfer tunnel on the ISS Zvezda module, citing concerns the work could worsen the persistent air leaks rather than fix them. The crack-riddled segment has lost pressure for years, and the two agencies remain at odds over the cause as the station nears its planned 2030 retirement.

title: Trump admin abandons fight against wind energy as clean energy output surges
blurb: The Trump administration dropped its appeal defending a January 2025 executive order that froze federal permitting and leasing for wind projects, after a district judge ruled the ban unlawful and "arbitrary and capricious." The retreat clears a major obstacle for the energy transition as a record 79.7 GW of clean power is projected to come online in the US in 2026.

title: How Millions of Digital Home Devices Are Secretly Powering Cyberattacks
blurb: Cheap consumer gadgets like routers, cameras, and smart-home devices are being quietly conscripted into proxy networks that route some of the most damaging cyberattacks on record. A WSJ investigation traces how foreign companies hijack these devices, masking attack traffic behind ordinary home IP addresses and leaving owners unaware their hardware is part of the operation.

title: State Attorneys General Are Investigating OpenAI
blurb: A coalition of state attorneys general has opened an investigation into OpenAI over a wide range of practices, including its handling of user data, protections for minors, and advertising activities. OpenAI disclosed the probe, which adds to mounting regulatory scrutiny as the company expands into consumer products and ad-supported features that depend on access to user information.

title: Siri AI Catching Up to Chatbot Competitors
blurb: Apple's revamped Siri, built on the company's AI overhaul, has improved enough to ease Apple out of its AI crisis, Bloomberg's Mark Gurman said. He noted the assistant mainly delivers on capabilities promised two years ago, leaving Apple still trailing chatbot rivals even as it narrows the gap.

title: Pentagon boasts of using AI to write reports mandated by Congress
blurb: Pentagon CTO Emil Michael told the Hudson Institute that the Department of Defense now uses generative AI to draft its hundreds of congressionally mandated annual reports, cutting one task from 200 hours of staffing to five. The tools run on GenAI.mil, the department's internal platform launched in December 2025 starting with Google Cloud's Gemini for Government, now available across all six military branches.

title: Judge Dismisses Elon Musk’s xAI Trade-Secret Lawsuit Against OpenAI
blurb: A federal judge dismissed Elon Musk's xAI lawsuit accusing OpenAI of stealing trade secrets by hiring away an xAI engineer who had worked on the company's chatbot. The ruling clears OpenAI of the poaching claim, marking another setback in Musk's broader legal campaign against the company he co-founded and now competes with through xAI.

title: Android 17 is about to make gaming on foldables way better
blurb: Android 17 introduces a foldable gaming mode that splits the screen 50/50, placing the game on the top half and a dynamic gamepad on the bottom to keep controls within reach without blocking the action. Google is also adding native controller remapping and improved memory cleanup to reduce frame drops, with the foldable features rolling out in the coming months.

title: SpaceX purchases Cursor, a Claude Code and OpenAI Codex competitor [U]
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in a roughly $60B deal, a week after the rocket company went public and following an earlier $10B partnership struck in April. The purchase strengthens a direct competitor to Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, giving Cursor access to xAI's Colossus infrastructure to scale training of its agentic coding models.

title: The team that built Microsoft’s Security Copilot just raised $100M to stop attacks before they happen
blurb: Ent, founded by two RiskIQ and Microsoft Security Copilot veterans, raised a $100M seed round led by Decibel, with Sequoia, Craft Ventures, Felicis, and the CIA's In-Q-Tel also participating. The startup runs small AI models locally on the endpoint to block attacks in under a second, a bet on prevention over the detect-and-clean-up model that dominates cybersecurity.

title: Marvell details vision of optically-interconnected data centers spanning across thousands of kilometers — new interconnects sampling later this year would allow CSPs to pool resources based on workload
blurb: At Computex 2026, Marvell CEO Matt Murphy detailed a plan for optical interconnects that pool compute and memory across data centers separated by thousands of kilometers, dissolving the campus boundaries set by copper's latency limits. The company's Colorz 1600 1.6 Tb/s coherent optical solution, built on a 2nm DSP for inter-data-center links, samples later this year, letting cloud providers allocate resources dynamically by workload.

title: Microsoft upgrades Surface Pro and Surface Laptop with Qualcomm Snapdragon X2 chips; Pro starts at $1,499 and Laptop at $1,599, both $100 higher than last-gen (Antonio G. Di Benedetto/The Verge)
blurb: Microsoft refreshed the Surface Pro and Surface Laptop with Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2 chips, with the Pro starting at $1,499 and the Laptop at $1,599. Both prices run $100 higher than the previous generation, continuing Microsoft's bet on Arm-based silicon for its consumer Surface line.

title: DARPA seeks swappable satellites to help with future star wars
blurb: DARPA issued a call for modular, swappable satellites designed to be reconfigured or upgraded in orbit, aimed at supporting future space-based defense systems. The agency wants spacecraft built from interchangeable components so payloads can be replaced without launching entirely new satellites, a shift toward treating orbital assets as serviceable hardware rather than single-mission vehicles.

title: Apple Plans Camera AirPods Alongside Upgraded Foldable iPhone in 2027
blurb: Apple plans to release camera-equipped AirPods in late 2027 as part of a wave of new hardware aimed at the AI device market. The launch will coincide with an upgraded foldable iPhone and a 20th-anniversary model, signaling Apple's push to build AI features into wearables beyond the phone.

title: The Adder at the Heart of Intel’s 8087 FPU
blurb: Ken Shirriff published a die-level analysis of the 69-bit adder at the core of Intel's 1980-era 8087 floating-point coprocessor, the circuit every operation from cosines to exponentiation ultimately depends on. The design splits addition into 4-bit blocks and uses a transistor-efficient Manchester carry-chain lookahead to avoid waiting on carries, a tradeoff Intel later abandoned for the faster Kogge-Stone adder.

title: Startup Backed by Ex-Google CEO Debuts Industrial Robot With LG
blurb: A startup backed by former Google CEO Eric Schmidt debuted an AI-powered industrial robot built in partnership with LG. The launch adds another well-funded entrant to the crowded humanoid and industrial robotics field, where investors are betting that AI control systems can finally make general-purpose machines viable on factory floors.

title: Trump Administration Reignites Its Feud With Anthropic Over Latest A.I. Models
blurb: The Trump administration imposed surprise restrictions Friday that cut off foreign access to Anthropic's latest AI models, reigniting a long-running feud between the two sides. The move triggered renewed finger-pointing over export policy and national security, adding pressure on Anthropic as it navigates Washington's tightening grip on who can use frontier models abroad.

title: Satya Nadella warns that AI could hollow out entire industries, echoing the damage done by globalization
blurb: Satya Nadella's essay warns that AI could centralize value in a few frontier models and hollow out entire industries.

title: Booster 20 Completes Cryo, As Ship 40 Nears Static Fire
blurb: SpaceX completed a cryogenic proof test on Booster 20 as Ship 40 moves toward its own static fire, advancing hardware for the next Starship flight following Flight 12 about three weeks ago. The parallel testing keeps both stages on track for an integrated launch, with the static fire the final major milestone before stacking and a flight readiness review.

title: A year after Meta tapped Alexandr Wang to build a new AI model, Zuckerberg has to sell it
blurb: Meta delivered its first proprietary foundation model, Muse Spark, in April, a year after spending $14.3B to bring in Alexandr Wang and his top Scale AI engineers to overhaul its AI efforts. The model gets Meta back on the map behind OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, but the stock is down 18% over the past year, and Wall Street wants proof Zuckerberg can monetize AI products beyond advertising.

title: Qualcomm takes spatial computing into the AI era with Snapdragon Reality Elite
blurb: Qualcomm released Snapdragon Reality Elite, a rebrand of its XR2+ Gen 2 chip that delivers up to 48 TOPS for on-device AI, enough to run 3B-parameter Llama models locally for translation and summarization. The renaming reflects Qualcomm's push to reposition its XR silicon as an edge-AI platform, betting that smarter on-device inference will drive the next wave of smart glasses and mixed-reality wearables.

title: Enthusiast hacks Valve’s AMD-first gaming OS to run on Intel hardware — SteamOS boots on Intel Arc B580 desktop GPU, but it takes a Radeon card, installer workaround, and Resizable BAR fix
blurb: An enthusiast got Valve's AMD-first SteamOS booting on an Intel Arc B580, but only via a Radeon card and installer workarounds.

title: Microsoft’s new Surface Pro 12 is its best 2-in-1 PC yet, but also its most expensive
blurb: Microsoft announced the Surface Pro 12, a 13-inch 2-in-1 built on Qualcomm's Snapdragon X2 chips that delivers up to 53% faster graphics and 15.5 hours of battery life. The base configuration now starts at $1,499, a $500 jump over the 11th Edition driven partly by industry-wide LPDDR5x RAM cost increases from the AI memory crisis.

title: Microsoft Surface Laptop 8 claims 20-hour battery life, improved GPU performance, and a steep $1,599 ask
blurb: Microsoft released the Surface Laptop 8, which runs on Qualcomm's new Snapdragon X2 chip and claims up to 20 hours of battery life and 58% better graphics performance than the prior generation. It starts at $1,599, roughly $500 above a comparably configured M5 MacBook Air, with Microsoft offsetting the gap through trade-in credit, a free Arc Mouse, and a discounted support plan.

title: MSI MPG Coreliquid P22 360 Review: Low noise, strong performance, budget price
blurb: MSI's MPG Coreliquid P22 360 delivers strong thermal performance, low noise, and a 2.1-inch IPS display for $129.99.

title: iOS 27 basically turns your iPhone and AirPods into an Apple Watch at the gym
blurb: With iOS 27, Apple is extending GymKit to iPhone and AirPods Pro 3, letting users tap compatible gym equipment to track workouts without an Apple Watch. AirPods Pro 3 supply heart-rate data while the machine sends back incline, pace, distance, and calories to the Fitness app, positioning the feature as a backup for watch owners and a sampler that could draw new Apple Watch buyers.

title: I admire Fabrice Bellard. He is almost certainly a better overall programmer
blurb: Carmack praises Fabrice Bellard, the FFmpeg and QEMU creator whose code quietly powers most internet video and virtualization.

title: VAST Data and the evolving neoclouds
blurb: VAST Data has become the dominant storage supplier to neoclouds, landing a 600 PB capacity win at Australia's Sharon AI that cements its lead in GPU-focused AI infrastructure. The neocloud sector collectively booked $23B to $25B in 2025 revenue, more than triple its 2024 total, as providers like CoreWeave expand into sovereign datacenters and managed services to challenge hyperscalers on enterprise AI inference.

title: OpenAI hit with sweeping probe from massive coalition of 42 US state attorneys general just days after reported IPO filing — subpoena targets ChatGPT maker’s ads, data practices, handling of minors, model sycophancy, and safety policies
blurb: A coalition of 42 US state attorneys general served OpenAI with a sweeping subpoena on June 12, targeting its advertising, data handling, treatment of minors, model sycophancy, and safety policies. The probe lands five days after OpenAI confidentially filed for an IPO that could value it at up to $1T, and adds to a civil suit Florida brought against the company and Sam Altman earlier this month.

title: A look at Specs, which have a battery life of up to four hours per charge, and an interview with Snap CEO Evan Spiegel about the AR glasses (Harry McCracken/Fast Company)
blurb: Snap detailed Specs, its upcoming consumer AR glasses, in an interview with CEO Evan Spiegel, with the standalone headset rated for up to four hours of battery life per charge. Spiegel positioned the device as Snap's bid to bring augmented reality to mainstream consumers, setting up a direct contest with Meta and Apple in the wearables race.

title: AMD buys data center memory optimization startup Mext
blurb: AMD acquired Mext, a startup whose AI-native memory tiering technology moves infrequently accessed data from costly DRAM to cheaper NAND flash, predicting and prefetching data back to DRAM before applications need it. The deal targets acute memory supply constraints in AI data centers, letting AMD customers run larger workloads on existing hardware while cutting DRAM spend and total cost of ownership.

title: Intel’s rumored Serpent Lake SoCs with Nvidia graphics may arrive in 2028
blurb: Intel is reportedly targeting Q1 2028 for Serpent Lake, an SoC that pairs Intel CPU cores with an Nvidia RTX graphics tile instead of the company's in-house Arc graphics. Per tipster FX57, the chips could debut at CES 2028 and would likely target AI PCs and high-end mobile workstations, marking a notable break from Intel's traditional strategy of building its own graphics.

title: Don't trust large context windows
blurb: LLMs reliably use only a fraction of their advertised context, with attention degrading well before the window fills and a practical "smart zone" cutoff around 100k tokens, a limit benchmarks like RULER and Chroma's context-rot report confirm. The author treats context as a budget, handing each new session a self-written spec or named artifact rather than relying on auto-compaction summaries produced by an already-degraded model.

title: This CarPlay change may be the missing link for Tesla support
blurb: Apple highlighted a CarPlay route-sharing feature in iOS 26.4 that lets navigation apps pass routing data to the vehicle, a capability seen as the likely missing piece for Tesla's rumored CarPlay support. The technology, surfaced during a WWDC developer session, matters for EVs because it ties charging stops and battery level into navigation, and it is available to developers now rather than in a future release.

title: Deep Space Network antenna mishap blamed on poor training and procedures
blurb: A NASA investigation blamed $4.1M to $4.6M in damage to its 70-meter DSS-14 Deep Space Network antenna at Goldstone on poor training and inadequate procedures, after the dish over-rotated and flooded its base with more than 750,000 liters of water and glycol. The report faulted reliance on "personal heroics" and undocumented institutional knowledge, and the antenna has been offline since the September 16 incident.

title: Apple explains why Siri’s major iOS 27 overhaul took so long
blurb: Apple's Siri lead Mike Rockwell said the iOS 27 Siri overhaul slipped because the company scrapped an early version that bolted tool calling onto the old Siri and rebuilt the assistant "from the ground up" on its new models. The result is a natively multimodal Siri with its own app that runs the same across iPhone, iPad, Mac, Watch, Vision Pro, CarPlay, and AirPods.

title: Snap's slimmed down AR Specs go on sale later this year for $2,195
blurb: Snap will sell its AR Specs to the public for $2,195 starting later this year, the first time its augmented reality glasses have been available outside a developer-only program. CEO Evan Spiegel showed off a slimmed-down design at 132 grams with a 51-degree field of view, four hours of battery life, and contextual AI assistance powered by OpenAI and Google partnerships.

title: iPhone 17 won’t get iOS 27’s top-tier features, here’s why
blurb: The base iPhone 17 won't get iOS 27's most powerful on-device AI model or Siri voice customization, both gated to 12GB RAM.

title: AWE 2026: Live updates from the Snap Specs launch by Evan Spiegel, and later XR keynotes at the show
blurb: Snap launched its new Specs AR glasses at AWE 2026, priced at $2,200, in a keynote led by CEO Evan Spiegel. Qualcomm and Google followed with Android XR updates, including a developer push for the $2,200-tier Xreal Aura and upcoming AI glasses from Warby Parker and Gentle Monster.

title: Intel's one-two punch plan in desktop CPUs is taking shape — Z990 spotted, Nova Lake detailed, ‘Raptor Lake Next’ teased
blurb: At Computex 2026 Intel detailed its desktop roadmap: Nova Lake at CES 2027, an LGA 1700 "Raptor Lake Next" refresh, and a 52-core flagship.

title: Finland charges Russian captain and crew member of ship suspected of damaging undersea cables — prosecutors claim ship had eight more targets before it was stopped by coast guard
blurb: Finland charged the Russian captain and Azerbaijani bosun of the cargo ship Fitburg with aggravated criminal mischief, alleging the vessel dragged its anchor over 80 miles and severed two undersea cables linking Finland and Estonia. Prosecutors say the ship intended to hit eight more cables before the coast guard stopped it, marking Finland's second sabotage prosecution after a 2024 case collapsed on jurisdictional grounds.

title: Commodore Releases Flip Phone
blurb: Commodore launched the Callback, a flip phone that runs messaging and music apps but blocks social media and browsers at the system level.

title: JAXA Nails H3 Return to Flight
blurb: JAXA launched its H3 rocket on its eighth mission Friday from Tanegashima Space Center, marking the first successful flight of the new three-engine variant. The vehicle delivered all six of its payloads to space, returning the program to flight after earlier setbacks and validating the upgraded configuration Japan plans to rely on for future launches.

title: Mechanical Watch (2022)
blurb: Bartosz Ciechanowski's interactive explainer breaks down how a purely mechanical watch keeps time, walking through the seven-part timekeeping system that converts stored spring energy into the steady rotation of the second hand. Draggable 3D models and animations let readers peek inside the movement, tracing power from the twisted spiral torsion spring through each gear and escapement without a single battery or electronic component.

title: Sustained maneuver has a propulsion problem
blurb: Spacecraft architecture has long been treated as a problem of placement, optimizing where to position satellites and how reliably they hold station. The author argues that emerging missions demand sustained maneuvering instead of fixed orbits, and that today's propulsion systems cannot supply the continuous thrust those operations require.

title: Here’s everything new for Apple Photos in iOS 27
blurb: iOS 27 brings AI editing tools (Extend, Reframe, upgraded Clean Up), full-resolution Shared Albums, and cross-platform sharing to Apple Photos.

title: Commodore gets into the phone biz with Sailfish-powered retro 'Callback'
blurb: Commodore is launching the Callback, a retro-styled smartphone running the Linux-based Sailfish OS instead of Android or iOS.

title: Apple faces yet another EU antitrust investigation, this time for preferencing iCloud
blurb: Italy's competition regulator opened a DMA investigation into whether Apple illegally preferences iCloud over rival cloud services.

title: Katalyst Space raises $12 million for GEO servicing demo mission
blurb: Katalyst Space Technologies raised $12M, led by Geodesic Capital, to build its first Nexus spacecraft for servicing satellites in geostationary orbit, with launch planned for 2027 on an Ariane 6. The round comes as Katalyst's Link spacecraft prepares for a late-June launch to attach to NASA's decaying Swift Observatory and raise its orbit, preventing an early reentry.

title: How Elon Musk's second-in-command Gwynne Shotwell helped turn SpaceX into an IPO giant
blurb: Gwynne Shotwell, SpaceX's president and COO and an early 2002 hire, rang the Nasdaq bell on Friday for the company's IPO and now holds a stake worth roughly $2 billion. She oversees SpaceX's 22,000-person workforce and its day-to-day operations, including Starlink and the xAI integration, while Musk sets high-level strategy and technical direction.

title: The Google / Xreal Aura XR glasses are now available to preorder
blurb: Xreal opened $99 reservations for the Aura, the second Android XR device co-developed with Google, ahead of a fall launch in the US, UK, Japan, Canada, and South Korea. The sub-95-gram glasses run on Qualcomm's new Snapdragon Reality Elite chip and Android XR, though Google has not disclosed a final price beyond a $199 launch credit for early reservations.

title: Insta360 Luna Ultra review: Let the gimbal camera wars begin
blurb: Insta360's Luna Ultra gimbal camera packs dual cameras, 3x optical zoom, a 1-inch sensor, and 8K video, breaking DJI's Osmo Pocket monopoly.

title: How Many Shots Are Enough for a Quantum Circuit?
blurb: A black-box framework, IncrementalExecution, decides when to stop running quantum circuit shots once added executions stop changing the output distribution.

title: A 'cold blob' in the Atlantic could be a sign of AMOC shutdown
blurb: A new study concludes the North Atlantic "cold blob" is driven by a weakening AMOC, a sign the current may be nearing collapse.

title: Commodore announces Linux-based flip phone with ‘no social media, no browser’ — the Callback 8020 will be available in five retro colorways starting at $499, runs 99% of Android apps
blurb: Commodore announced the Callback 8020, a Linux-based flip phone that hard-blocks browsers, social media, and work apps to encourage a digital detox. Built on a custom version of Jolla's Sailfish OS, it still runs 99% of Android apps and starts at $499, with pre-orders opening June 30th across five retro colorways.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: NVIDIA's engineering blog details advanced fusion kernels that increase training throughput for mixture-of-experts (MoE) models by combining multiple operations into single GPU kernels to cut memory traffic and launch overhead. The post covers fused kernels for routing, grouped GEMMs, and activation functions, showing measurable speedups for the expert layers that dominate MoE compute.
