blurb: Mobileye plans to launch its own US robotaxi service in 2027, putting the AV supplier in direct competition with the automakers it sells self-driving systems to, including Volkswagen. It starts with 100 vehicles phased in across one unnamed city, then aims to scale to roughly 17,000 robotaxis over the following five years. CEO Amnon Shashua has long argued the operator business is the only path to consumer autonomy, and the move signals that owning the fleet, not just the stack, is where the supplier sees the margin.

title: SpaceX Is Now More Valuable Than Amazon, Meta and Many Others
blurb: SpaceX vaulted into the world's five most valuable public companies within its first few days of trading, passing Amazon and Meta. The listing turns a privately held bet on rockets and Starlink into one of the largest market caps on the planet, built on launch dominance and a satellite internet business now reaching millions. For founders, it marks the moment hard-tech moats started commanding software multiples, a signal that capital is willing to pay up for businesses that are physically hard to replicate.

title: SpaceX Set to Overtake Amazon in Value as It Soars for Third Day
blurb: SpaceX is on track to pass Amazon as the world's fifth-largest public company, with shares up more than 50% across three sessions since its debut. The run puts a launch-and-satellite business above one of the defining platforms of the internet era, on the strength of Starlink's recurring revenue and Starship's payload economics. The market is now pricing space infrastructure as a core franchise rather than a moonshot, which resets what a credible hard-tech valuation looks like for every founder raising in the category.

title: Letter: Hightouch, an ad tech startup recently valued at $2.75B, offers to buy LiveRamp's identity business from Publicis for $800M to $1.2B in cash and stock (Sara Fischer/Axios)
blurb: Hightouch, an ad tech startup recently valued at $2.75B, has offered $800M to $1.2B in cash and stock to buy LiveRamp's identity business from Publicis, which acquired LiveRamp only months earlier. The bid signals that identity resolution, the connective layer that ties customer data to ad targeting, is becoming the asset everyone in the stack wants to own as third-party cookies fade. For founders building on top of customer data, the consolidation means the infrastructure underneath you is changing hands fast, and your data partners may soon answer to a competitor.

title: ChapsVision to replace Palantir in major contract with French intelligence agency
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency is dropping Palantir and handing the contract to ChapsVision, a homegrown French data-analysis firm. The move is the latest sign that European governments increasingly treat dependence on US software as a strategic liability, not just a procurement choice. For founders building in defense, govtech, or any regulated sector, sovereignty is becoming a feature buyers will pay for, and incumbents with the deepest tech can still lose on where their code is written and who controls it.

title: Dan Ives on US-Anthropic Talks, AI M&A, Open AI Losses
blurb: Wedbush analyst Dan Ives expects AI dealmaking to accelerate sharply, framing a regulatory "tug of war" between Anthropic and the Trump administration as the backdrop. He points to mounting losses at OpenAI as the company heads toward an IPO later this year. The signal for founders is that consolidation is coming, and the labs everyone builds on are negotiating their position with Washington while still bleeding cash at scale.

title: Malaysia’s AI agent-powered messaging app Respond.io raises $62.5M, eyes acquisitions
blurb: Respond.io, one of Malaysia's startups to watch, raised $62.5M to fund acquisitions across North America and Europe. The messaging platform runs AI agents that handle high volumes of customer inquiries and charges per conversation rather than per seat, a pricing model that ties cost to resolved work instead of headcount. The round signals that the per-conversation model is starting to pull capital toward emerging-market AI infrastructure, and that consolidation in the customer-messaging space is now being driven from outside Silicon Valley.

title: Anthropic Dispatches Staff to D.C., Racing to Resolve AI Export Restrictions
blurb: Anthropic is sending staff to Washington to negotiate an end to export restrictions that forced it to shut down its most powerful models in affected markets. The move signals how much frontier-model access now depends on policy outcomes rather than engineering, with a single regulatory decision capable of cutting off entire regions overnight. For founders building on these labs, it's a reminder that your supplier's geopolitical exposure is now your own, and the availability you price against can change by government order.

title: Self-driving tech supplier Mobileye targets U.S. robotaxi launch in 2027
blurb: Mobileye, the self-driving tech supplier, said it will launch its own U.S. robotaxi service in 2027, starting with a 100-vehicle fleet in a major city and scaling toward roughly 17,000 vehicles over five years. The move puts Mobileye in direct competition with the automakers that license its Mobileye Drive platform, even as it calls the service a complementary path to market. It enters a field where Waymo already operates in 11 U.S. cities, while Zoox, Tesla, and others race to expand, so a parts supplier turning operator signals how crowded the robotaxi stack is getting.

title: Payments startup Flutterwave hits $3.2B valuation, backed by Ripple
blurb: Flutterwave raised a Series E that values the African payments company at $3.2B, with Ripple taking an equity stake to expand digital asset infrastructure across the continent. The bet is that crypto rails route around Africa's core payments problem, where fragmented banking, FX controls, and transactions detouring through London make cross-border settlement slow and expensive. Flutterwave now operates in 35 countries, has bought banking startup Mono for its API tech, and added stablecoin payments with Polygon, all aimed at making the continent function as one market rather than dozens of disconnected ones.

title: Carvana is expanding into new vehicles. The implications could reshape the U.S. automotive retail market
blurb: Carvana, the largest online used-car retailer, has quietly bought seven new-vehicle franchises selling Stellantis brands since last year and is now moving into the new car market. Its first Stellantis store in Casa Grande, Arizona sold more than 700 vehicles last month to become the bestselling Stellantis location in the country, up from 30 to 50 monthly sales before the acquisition. Owning new-car franchises also feeds Carvana's core business, giving it trade-ins from new buyers and access to dealer-only auctions, which analysts say could reshape a franchised dealer system that booked $1.3 trillion in sales last year.

title: The hidden pattern behind successful products | Mark Pincus (founder of Zynga)
blurb: Successful products almost never come from a genuinely new idea. Mark Pincus argues your instincts about what to build are usually right, but the specific idea is usually wrong, so the move is to start from something already proven and make it meaningfully better before reaching for something new. His "Proven, Better, New" framework puts the odds in that order: take a validated category, find the dimension where you can win, and treat pure novelty as the riskiest bet rather than the goal.

title: US battery manufacturing output continues to break records
blurb: US battery manufacturing output hit another all-time high, extending a run of records that has roughly doubled domestic production over the past few years. The surge tracks a wave of new gigafactories coming online, much of it underwritten by IRA-era subsidies and the push to onshore EV and grid-storage supply chains. For founders, the signal is that the buildout is no longer a forecast but a measurable industrial base, and the next layer of value moves to the software, materials, and grid-integration companies that ride on top of it.

title: State Attorneys General Are Investigating OpenAI
blurb: A coalition of state attorneys general has opened an investigation into OpenAI, probing its handling of user data, the safety of minors, and its move into advertising. The scope matters because it spans the parts of the business OpenAI is leaning on for growth, from the consumer data that trains and personalizes its products to the ad model it is just starting to build. Regulatory pressure at the state level tends to move faster and more unpredictably than federal action, and it lands on every company building on top of OpenAI's platform too.

title: Radical Numerics launches with $50M to build general biological intelligence
blurb: Radical Numerics launched with $50M to build what it calls general biological intelligence, models that learn directly across DNA, RNA, and proteins instead of one strand at a time. The team is the group behind Evo, the open-source model that read and wrote DNA and was later used to generate the first complete AI-designed genome. The bet is that a single multimodal system reasoning across all of biology unlocks cancer diagnostics, drug target discovery, and biosecurity, the same capability that lowers the barrier to designing harmful pathogens, which is why the company is building detection alongside design.

title: IT hurtles toward the ‘Great Enterprise Pricing Reset’
blurb: Enterprise software pricing is splitting into two models as AI products force vendors to abandon per-seat billing. Outcome-based pricing generally favors CIOs, but consumption-based billing transfers forecasting risk onto buyers and can produce invoice surprises that most teams have never had to manage. Gartner expects at least 40% of enterprise SaaS spend to shift to usage, agent, or outcome pricing by 2030, with seat-based revenue share falling from 21% to 15%. If you sell software, hybrid pricing is the likely default until agent adoption settles which model actually wins.

title: China's supreme court bans Infineon from selling GaN power chips in China — market-leader Innoscience secures major victory in multi-region patent war
blurb: China's Supreme People's Court upheld an injunction barring Infineon from selling the disputed GaN power chips in mainland China, the final word in a patent fight brought by market leader Innoscience. Both companies sit on Nvidia's approved supplier list for the 800V power delivery that AI racks pushing past 200kW now depend on, so the litigation is carving up who can ship the underlying silicon by region. Each side has already won in a different jurisdiction, with parallel cases running in the US and Germany, and the practical result is that the GaN supply chain for AI data centers is fracturing along legal lines.

title: The team that built Microsoft’s Security Copilot just raised $100M to stop attacks before they happen
blurb: Ent, founded by the RiskIQ veterans who built Microsoft's Security Copilot, came out of stealth on 16 June with a $100M seed led by Decibel, one of the largest seed rounds in cybersecurity history. The bet is that small AI models can now run locally on the endpoint, making a block-or-allow decision in under a second, before an action completes. That reverses the industry's decade-long retreat from prevention into detection, when cloud round trips were too slow to stop an attack in real time. Backers include Sequoia, Craft Ventures, Felicis, and the CIA's In-Q-Tel.

title: Satya Nadella warns that AI could hollow out entire industries, echoing the damage done by globalization
blurb: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella published an essay arguing that the defining economic risk of the AI era is a handful of frontier models absorbing the expertise of entire industries and commoditizing it, stripping companies of their moats. He frames the answer around two assets, "human capital" and "token capital," and says the win is not picking the best model but building a learning loop on top of models where the two compound. The implication for founders is that durable advantage will live in proprietary data and workflow loops, not in whichever model you happen to call this quarter.

title: GameStop Investor Brings Suit Over CEO’s $35 Billion Pay Package
blurb: A GameStop shareholder is suing to block a vote on a $35B pay package for the company's CEO until shareholders get proper disclosures. The size is the story, dwarfing even the $56B Musk award that Delaware courts twice struck down, and it lands at a company whose market cap doesn't come close to justifying it. The fight signals that mega grants tied to meme-stock momentum will draw the same legal scrutiny as Tesla's, and boards writing outsized founder packages should expect disclosure challenges before the vote, not after.

title: OpenAI hit with sweeping probe from massive coalition of 42 US state attorneys general just days after reported IPO filing — subpoena targets ChatGPT maker’s ads, data practices, handling of minors, model sycophancy, and safety policies
blurb: A coalition of 42 state attorneys general served OpenAI a sweeping subpoena on June 12, demanding documents on its advertising, data practices, handling of minors, model sycophancy, and safety policies. The timing matters: it landed five days after OpenAI confidentially filed for an IPO that reportedly values the company near $1 trillion, and weeks after Florida sued OpenAI and Sam Altman directly. For founders, it signals that regulators now treat consumer AI like a product-liability category, and the scrutiny is arriving exactly as the biggest player tries to go public.

title: Linux 7.1
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, the latest mainline kernel, following the standard two-week stabilization period after the final release candidate. As with past major-number bumps, the jump carries no special significance beyond keeping minor version numbers from growing unwieldy, with the bulk of the changes landing as routine driver updates, filesystem fixes, and incremental subsystem improvements.

title: Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages
blurb: Attackers compromised roughly 1,500 packages in the Arch User Repository, embedding the Atomic infostealer in builds pulled by Arch Linux users. The project halted new account registrations to stem the flood of malicious uploads, a reminder that user-submitted package repos like AUR ship without the vetting of official channels.

title: Attackers hijacked over 1,500 Arch Linux packages to steal developers’ secrets, no hacking required
blurb: Attackers hijacked more than 1,500 packages in the Arch User Repository, adopting orphaned packages and rewriting their build scripts to pull in a credential-stealing npm package that ran a hidden binary at compile time. The campaign, dubbed "Atomic Arch," targeted developers specifically, harvesting GitHub and npm tokens, SSH keys, Docker logins, Slack and Vault credentials, and shipping them out over Tor while Arch's official repositories stayed untouched.

title: Phoenix LiveView 1.2
blurb: Phoenix LiveView 1.2 introduces colocated hooks and colocated JS, which let developers write client-side JavaScript directly inside HEEx templates instead of maintaining separate hook files, with the JS extracted at compile time and bundled by the asset pipeline. The release also adds a new portal API for rendering content outside the current DOM tree, useful for modals and tooltips, and ships compatibility fixes for the latest Phoenix and Elixir versions.

title: From Third-Party to First-Party: Measuring and Protecting Against Modern Web Tracking Mechanisms
blurb: A large-scale measurement of top sites found that over 54% now deploy first-party or server-side tracking, which relocates tracking logic into the browser's first-party context or the website's backend to evade traditional client-side detection. The authors build a provider-independent detection methodology and cluster scripts by similarity into a network graph, showing the tracking ecosystem persists even as it dodges conventional privacy tools.

title: Arch Linux locks down AUR signups amid wave of malicious commits
blurb: Arch Linux froze new account signups on the Arch User Repository after attackers flooded it with malicious commits that poisoned package updates. The community-maintained AUR ships build scripts rather than vetted binaries, so the lockdown buys maintainers time to clean compromised packages and review the backlog before reopening registration to contributors.

title: SPARK: Security Knowledge Priming and Representation-Guided Knowledge Activation for LLM-based Secure Code Generation
blurb: SPARK is an inference-time security harness that improves LLM secure code generation by retrieving relevant CWE entries and appending a short structured cue to the prompt, activating latent security knowledge without any retraining. The authors argue pretraining corpora already contain rich security material and that the real bottleneck is activation, positioning SPARK against heavier defenses like fine-tuning or external knowledge retrieval that add compute overhead and data bias.

title: Open-SWE-Traces: Advancing Dual-Mode Multilingual Distillation for Software Engineering Agents
blurb: Open-SWE-Traces is a dataset of 207,489 agentic coding trajectories spanning nine languages, sourced from 20,000 real-world PRs via OpenHands and SWE-agent and synthesized with both explicit-reasoning and non-thinking traces. Fine-tuning the Qwen3-30B-A3B series on the data yields resolve rates of 61.7% on SWE-bench Verified, 57.1% on SWE-bench Multilingual, and 36.8% on SWE-bench Pro.

title: Chrome is removing the last workaround keeping Manifest V2 ad blockers alive
blurb: Chrome 150, shipping June 30, removes the final developer flag that let Manifest V2 extensions like uBlock Origin keep running in the browser. The change completes Google's multi-year migration to Manifest V3, whose declarativeNetRequest API caps filter rules and blocks the dynamic request interception that powerful ad and tracker blockers depend on.

title: LLM-as-Code Agentic Programming for Agent Harness
blurb: This paper argues that token explosion, control-flow hallucination, and unreliable completion in LLM agents are architectural consequences of making a probabilistic model orchestrate deterministic looping, branching, and sequencing. The authors propose Agentic Programming, where the program owns all control flow and the LLM is invoked as an adaptive component only for reasoning or generation, with each call's context built from the execution history's call tree as a DAG.

title: Configuration Smells in AGENTS.md Files: Common Mistakes in Configuring Coding Agents
blurb: This paper presents the first catalog of "configuration smells" in AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md files, the instruction files that steer coding agents on architecture, conventions, and testing. After mining repositories and reviewing 100 popular open-source projects, the authors define six smells with automated detection heuristics, finding Lint Leakage (linter rules duplicated into agent instructions) the most common at 62% of files.

title: did:crdt: Coordination-Free Decentralised Identifiers via Signed CRDTs
blurb: did:crdt is a W3C DID Core method that drops ledgers, sequencers, and global ordering by composing each DID document field from signed CRDTs that merge deterministically under causal delivery. The paper gives the threat model, CRUD and conflict-resolution semantics, and a Rust reference implementation with property-based convergence tests and microsecond-scale merge latency, with the trade-off that unauthenticated state-merge is a trusted-domain optimization while Byzantine safety holds only on signed-delta paths.

title: Snyk VulnBench JS 1.0: Can LLMs Find the Same Bugs Twice?
blurb: This benchmark ran 300 repeated agentic LLM security scans on identical JavaScript, prompts, and harness, finding that reference-matched vulnerabilities were highly repeatable (134 of 158 appeared in all five runs) while unmatched extra findings were not, with 80 of 161 surfacing in only one of five repetitions. The authors argue LLM review and Snyk's deterministic SAST are complementary: models reliably catch high-signal exploit shapes, while static analysis is better at systematically enumerating repeated data-flow sinks.

title: obliv-clang: Real-World Oblivious Programming in C++
blurb: obliv-clang is a compile-time checker that verifies the obliviousness of C++ programs, ensuring execution time and data access patterns leak no secrets to timing and access-pattern side-channel attacks. It supports rich C++ features including arbitrarily nested pointers to work with existing industry codebases, ships formally proven soundness rules, and produces high-performance binaries with minimal compilation overhead.

title: Honda Civics and Installing Software With Android Test Keys
blurb: Eric McDonald discovered that the Android-based infotainment head unit in a 2021 Honda Civic can be updated over USB using stock AOSP test keys left on the file system, letting anyone with physical access run arbitrary signed code. The write-up, which he calls the EvilValet attack, extends his 2023 reverse-engineering work and notes the flaw likely affects other cars since these third-party units get reused across generations.

title: Q-READY: Predictive Feasibility Assessment for Hybrid Quantum-Classical Applications
blurb: Q-READY proposes a Model-Based Systems Engineering pipeline for assessing whether a hybrid quantum-classical solution is feasible before teams sink cost into implementation, covering requirements modeling, problem formulation, workflow design, and hardware-aware evaluation against real backend constraints like qubit scarcity and noise. The authors walk through a credit-portfolio capital-assessment example, linking strategy choices and backend assumptions into traceable system-level models that simulate and compare candidate designs.

title: How to Make AI Agents Follow Your Design System
blurb: The author argues that AI agents fail to follow design systems because they imitate what a codebase actually does, not what its rules files claim, so agents reliably copy legacy imports, deprecated hooks, and hardcoded colors that outnumber their correct replacements. He contends that ever-larger .cursorrules and AGENTS.md files don't fix this, since long prose instructions degrade in context, rot silently, and always lose to the patterns models infer directly from code.

title: Organizational Cohesion in Microservice Architectures: A Multi-Project Empirical Study
blurb: This paper introduces organizational cohesion, a measure of how closely developer contributions align with microservice boundaries, operationalized as Pairwise Team Cohesion (PTC) by adapting the Sensitive Class Cohesion Metric. The authors validate it through a longitudinal study of Netflix's Spinnaker platform and six other open-source microservice systems, finding systematic divergences between service decomposition and the team structures that actually maintain those services.

title: Rate-limiting anonymous users with no login, no Redis — just a cookie and an IP
blurb: The author rate-limits anonymous users to 3 calls per day without Redis or login, combining an httpOnly cookie with the client IP and counting rows in an existing MongoDB. Each signal covers the other's blind spot, since cookies reset in incognito and IPs are shared behind NAT, and the post is upfront about the failure modes that still leak through before reaching for heavier infrastructure.

title: Accelerate search queries with full-text search indexes on Databricks
blurb: Databricks added full-text search indexes that accelerate keyword and substring queries on large tables, letting teams filter hundreds of gigabytes to terabytes without full scans. The indexes integrate directly into Delta tables, so search-heavy workloads like log analysis and document lookup run on the lakehouse instead of requiring a separate search engine like Elasticsearch.

title: Databricks Sales Double for Product That Competes With Snowflake
blurb: Databricks said its data warehousing business more than doubled over the past year to a $1.5B annual run rate, putting its SQL product in direct competition with Snowflake and Google BigQuery. The growth shows the lakehouse vendor narrowing the gap on the warehouse-native workloads that have long been Snowflake's core, as both companies converge on Iceberg-backed storage and overlapping compute.

title: Unifying Data and Governance in the Agentic Era: What’s New with Azure Databricks
blurb: At Data + AI Summit 2026, Databricks detailed a set of Azure Databricks updates centered on unifying data and governance through Unity Catalog as agentic workloads move into production. The push positions Unity Catalog as the single permission and lineage layer spanning tables, models, and AI agents, addressing the governance gap teams hit when agents start reading and writing across the Azure data estate.

title: Phantoms and Disclosures: a Causal Framework for Auditing Synthetic Data
blurb: This paper presents an auditing framework for detecting when synthetic data leaks private information from its training corpus. The key contribution is a causal distinction between "true disclosures," where the system directly reproduces a user's record, and "phantom disclosures," where it incidentally generates matching data by chance. By splitting inputs into training and holdout sets and running statistical hypothesis tests against zero-learning or differential privacy baselines, the method audits disclosure risk with no model access and no canary insertion required.

title: Introducing Genie One, Genie Agents, and Genie Ontology
blurb: Databricks released Genie One, a unified conversational analytics interface, alongside Genie Agents for multi-step autonomous analysis and Genie Ontology, a semantic layer that grounds queries in defined business metrics and entity relationships. The ontology is the key piece for data teams, encoding the metric definitions and join logic that text-to-SQL tools usually guess at, which should cut the hallucinated joins and inconsistent numbers that have kept Genie out of production reporting.

title: When Does q-error Predict Plan Regret? Three Regimes of Cardinality-Estimation Error
blurb: This paper challenges the field's reliance on q-error as a proxy for query-plan quality, offering a measurement-driven account of when it holds. The authors identify three regimes: for small estimation errors, a true-point condition number predicts plan regret and beats q-error; for the large errors where learned estimators actually operate, an estimator-independent ACS-infinity measure flags regret-prone queries (Spearman rho ~0.54 on STATS-CEB) while q-error is nearly useless at the query level (rho ~0.05).

title: HAMON: Passive Optical Sequence Mixing for Long-Horizon Forecasting
blurb: HAMON replaces the learned digital sequence-mixing layer in time-series forecasting with a passive diffractive optical core, encoding history onto an optical aperture and using cascaded trainable phase masks to shape the forecast directly in the output field. The bet is that long-horizon forecasting operators are often low-complexity and approximately linear, so they don't need dense transformer-style superposition. At inference, prediction is a single passive optical propagation pass with no digital mixing, and the authors report it beating the strongest digital baselines on ETTm2.

title: Free SQL→ER diagram tool, runs in the browser, nothing uploaded
blurb: A free browser-based tool converts SQL schema definitions into entity-relationship diagrams, with all parsing done client-side so nothing is uploaded to a server. The local-only approach lets data teams diagram production schemas without sending DDL to a third party, and the tool reads standard CREATE TABLE statements to map tables, columns, and foreign-key relationships.

title: What’s new in data agents: Supercharging your AI workflows
blurb: Google expanded its Agentic Data Cloud with new data agents, led by Conversational Analytics now in preview across BigQuery, Lakehouse, AlloyDB, Spanner, and Cloud SQL, letting teams query data in natural language and grounding answers in enterprise context. The Lakehouse integration queries distributed lakes across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud without moving data, while BigQuery's reasoning engine automates root-cause analysis directly in BigQuery Studio.

title: UXBench: Measuring the Actionability of LLM-Generated UX Critiques
blurb: UXBench introduces a benchmark for evaluating LLMs as UX judges, pairing ten families of runnable web fixtures with coverage-gated browser exploration that forces models to gather interaction evidence before critiquing. Report quality is scored by whether a downstream repair agent can actually fix the interface, and across eight frontier models the results show UX judging is neither saturated nor one-dimensional.

title: Beyond Usability: A UX Case Study on Using "Withdrawal Design" to Challenge Engagement Metrics in Social Robotics
blurb: A randomized 8-week home trial of a social robot for autistic children found that keeping the robot lowered anxiety but reduced parent-reported social motivation and emotion-recognition gains versus children who had it withdrawn. The authors argue UX researchers should stop treating engagement and usability as proxies for success, since high SUS scores can mask ecological downsides for vulnerable users.

title: What’s !important #13: @function, alpha(), CSS Wordle, and More
blurb: The latest issue of CSS-Tricks' What's !important roundup covers the new `@function` rule for authoring custom CSS functions, the `alpha()` function for adjusting opacity on existing colors, and Grid Lanes for more flexible track placement. It also surfaces lesser-known `dialog` element behaviors and a CSS-only Wordle build worth studying for layout tricks.

title: MVRDV tops "polite yet radical" Eindhoven housing with angular roofs
blurb: MVRDV completed Nieuw Bergen in Eindhoven, 237 mixed-tenure homes across seven blocks topped with jagged grass-covered roofs.

title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
blurb: Defused Cyber reports active exploitation of three Fortinet FortiSandbox vulnerabilities (CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089) over the past 24 hours, including a critical path traversal flaw in the JRPC API (CVE-2026-39813, CVSS 9.1) that Fortinet patched only last week. Operators running FortiSandbox appliances should apply the latest fixes immediately and hunt for path-traversal indicators against the JRPC API, treating exposed instances as potentially already compromised.

title: China-Linked SprySOCKS Backdoor Expands to Windows with Driver-Based Stealth
blurb: ESET identified two previously undocumented Windows variants of SprySOCKS, a backdoor tied to China-linked activity and once thought to be Linux-only, internally marked WIN_DRV and WIN_PLUS. Both ship with hard-coded C&C configurations and communicate over TCP and UDP, with the WIN_DRV variant using a kernel driver to hide its presence on infected hosts.

title: Cisco patches SD-WAN flaw amid evidence of active exploitation
blurb: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262, a flaw in the web interface of Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (formerly vManage) that lets an authenticated attacker with write access upload crafted files to overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root, after observing limited exploitation in the wild. There are no workarounds, so admins should upgrade to fixed releases and review SD-WAN Manager logs for uploads of index.jsp or .war files.

title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-54420 (CVSS 8.5), a privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after confirming active exploitation for root access on affected hosts. FCEB agencies must remediate by June 18, 2026; operators running the plugin should patch immediately and audit for signs of root-level compromise on shared hosting environments.

title: Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw
blurb: Palo Alto Networks confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS 7.8), an authentication-bypass flaw in the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS, by an unknown threat actor gaining unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals. Organizations running affected PAN-OS versions on internet-facing GlobalProtect should patch immediately, audit portal access logs for unauthorized sessions, and rotate credentials on appliances that may already be compromised.

title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed "SearchLeak," a now-patched three-stage attack against Microsoft 365 Copilot that used parameter-to-prompt injection (P2P) to silently exfiltrate emails, OneDrive files, SharePoint documents, and MFA codes via a single malicious Copilot link. The attack bypassed guardrails and browser CSP by hiding the exfiltration URL in an image tag behind a Bing search-by-image link, triggering a server-side fetch from Bing's infrastructure before Microsoft could sanitize the prompt.

title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
blurb: Rokarolla, a new Android banking Trojan tracked by Zimperium zLabs, spreads via fake Chrome and TikTok download pages and steals credentials from 217 banking and crypto apps. Beyond theft, it wields 137 C2 commands for full device control, harvesting lock-screen credentials, contacts, SMS, and keystrokes while blocking calls, deploying overlays, and disabling Google Play Protect.

title: Your "Pro" LLM Subscription May Actually Be "Free": Exposing Fingerprint Spoofing Risks in LLM Inference Services
blurb: Researchers introduced fingerprint spoofing, a threat in which a malicious LLM API provider serves a cheaper, weaker model that has been parameter-efficiently fine-tuned to mimic a premium one, evading the black-box fingerprinting users rely on to verify they get the advertised model. Their GhostPrint framework combines surrogate modeling, reward-ranked fine-tuning, and knowledge distillation to defeat fingerprinting under realistic query-budget and classifier constraints in both static and continual settings.

title: Cybercrime Group Claims Novo Nordisk Hack
blurb: Hack-and-leak group FulcrumSec claimed it breached Novo Nordisk in March via a leaked GitHub access token, cloning repositories and harvesting additional credentials to exfiltrate roughly 1.3TB including undisclosed drug programs, proprietary compound structures, the Dicerna RNAi pipeline, and private AI models. The group demanded $25M, and after the failed extortion it is now threatening to leak the pseudonymized clinical-trial data Novo Nordisk disclosed last week.

title: DoubtProbe: Black-Box Jailbreak Defense via Structural Verification and Semantic Auditing
blurb: DoubtProbe is a black-box jailbreak defense that reframes detection as consistency checking under controlled transformation, on the observation that most jailbreaks reorganize a harmful goal rather than remove it. A structural branch reconstructs the request from a normalized representation while a semantic branch audits intent, flagging inputs whose meaning shifts across transformation. The approach targets evolving prompt packaging and structure manipulation that known-attack coverage and prompt-level judgment tend to miss.

title: Cisco discloses second exploited SD-WAN vulnerability in two weeks (CVE-2026-20262)
blurb: Cisco disclosed CVE-2026-20262, a path traversal flaw in the Catalyst SD-WAN Manager web UI that lets an authenticated attacker with write access create or overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root, marking the second exploited SD-WAN Manager bug in two weeks. PSIRT has observed in-the-wild exploitation dropping a malicious .war file that WildFly deploys as a web shell, so upgrade to a fixed release and hunt the advisory's IOCs in exposed systems.

title: From Third-Party to First-Party: Measuring and Protecting Against Modern Web Tracking Mechanisms
blurb: A new study measuring top sites found that over 54% now deploy first-party tracking (FPT) or server-side tracking (SST), relocating tracking logic into the browser's first-party context or the site's backend to evade client-side detection. The authors introduce a provider-independent detection method and use script-similarity clustering to map the ecosystem, giving defenders and privacy tooling a way to spot tracking that traditional blocklists and client-side tools now miss.

title: MIPSBLEED: Uncovering Microarchitectural Timing Leaks in Pervasive Embedded Processors
blurb: MIPSBLEED demonstrates practical cross-core timing attacks on SMT-enabled MIPS processors used in routers, industrial controllers, and IoT devices, leaking data from the L1 data cache, L1 instruction cache, and execution engine without privileged access. The researchers chained the channels into a single-trace key recovery against a real elliptic curve cryptography toolkit, putting any embedded MIPS deployment running SMT and crypto workloads at risk.

title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
blurb: A China-linked espionage group lived inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks for over a year, deploying a backdoor on REDCap servers to harvest login credentials and exfiltrate sensitive research and defense email. Rather than using malware to pull data out, the attackers rewrote victims' own Google Workspace mail-forwarding rules to silently copy every message to attacker-controlled inboxes, evading endpoint detection.

title: Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims
blurb: California Water Service is investigating claims by Handala, an Iran-linked threat actor widely assessed as a front for Iranian state operations, which leaked 5 GB of data allegedly stolen in retaliation for recent US strikes on Iran. Threat-intel firm Dataminr found the dump includes customer PII from a compromised billing database and Cal Water's internal RTKBase application; the utility reports no known operational disruption to water or wastewater systems.

title: FBI dismantles Chinese phishing service that coached buyers to generate scam sites using AI —$88 cybercrime product linked to $1.9 billion in losses, 3.87 million stolen cards
blurb: The FBI, Google, and Lumen Technologies dismantled Outsider Enterprise, a China-based phishing-as-a-service operation sold via a Telegram bot for $88 per week, seizing its servers and payment wallets under Operation Ghost Hook. Google's complaint alleges operators distributed tutorials coaching subscribers to bypass Gemini's safety filters by framing phishing-page code as an innocuous "gift redemption page," and ties the platform to 3.87 million stolen cards and $1.9 billion in losses since July 2023.

title: Invisible Manipulation Channels in AI-Assisted Financial Advisory: Implications for Market Integrity and Regulatory Design
blurb: This paper identifies an "invisible manipulation channel" at the sampling layer of LLM inference that lets adversaries systematically bias AI-generated credit ratings and investment advice while passing output-based audits, including statistical watermarking. The authors show the manipulated and normal output distributions can be made arbitrarily close in KL divergence, so any output-based detector needs impractically large sample sizes to catch it reliably.

title: iRhythm discloses data breach, says hackers stole patient info
blurb: iRhythm Holdings disclosed in an SEC filing that a threat actor exfiltrated patient protected health information and other personal data from third-party-hosted business applications, gaining initial access through social engineering. The company confirmed the exfiltration after receiving a June 9 ransom demand, deemed the incident material given the data volume, and said its clinical and medical device systems were not affected.

title: Attackers hijacked over 1,500 Arch Linux packages to steal developers’ secrets, no hacking required
blurb: Attackers seized more than 1,500 orphaned packages in the Arch User Repository and rewrote their build scripts to pull in the malicious npm package atomic-lockfile, whose install hook ran a Rust credential stealer at build time. Dubbed "Atomic Arch" by Sonatype, the campaign harvests browser cookies, Slack and Teams sessions, GitHub, npm, Vault, and OpenAI tokens, SSH keys, and Docker logins; Arch's official repos were unaffected, but maintainers froze new account registration during cleanup.

title: 'Lorem Ipsum' Malware Pivots to ClickFix Delivery
blurb: The operators of the Lorem Ipsum shellcode loader have abandoned Trojanized Microsoft Teams installers for ClickFix lures hosted on compromised WordPress sites, after Microsoft's takedown of signing-as-a-service provider Fox Tempest revoked over 1,000 fraudulent Trusted Signing certificates. BlueVoyant now links the campaign to Rapid Brigantine (aka Vanilla Tempest, Vice Society), broadening the victim pool from installer downloaders to anyone browsing the infected sites.

title: Dynamic Malicious Skills in Agentic AI
blurb: This paper demonstrates "dynamic malicious skills," an attack in which instructions embedded in natural-language skill documentation such as SKILL.md induce an agent to inject malicious logic into an otherwise benign skill at runtime, validated against OpenHands and Claude Code with non-trivial success rates. The authors propose an OS-level defense using kernel-enforced read-only mounts to block runtime skill modification while preserving benign skill functionality.

title: SPARK: Security Knowledge Priming and Representation-Guided Knowledge Activation for LLM-based Secure Code Generation
blurb: SPARK is an inference-time security harness that reduces LLM-generated code vulnerabilities without retraining, arguing the security knowledge is already latent in pretraining and only needs activation. It retrieves relevant CWE entries per coding task and appends a short structured cue to the prompt, surfacing the model's existing safety representations. The approach sidesteps the compute overhead and data bias of fine-tuning or heavy retrieval-augmented defenses.

title: Transferable Self-Evolving Playbooks for Agentic Security Auditing
blurb: EvoHunt automates audit playbook creation for LLM security agents, replacing human-curated prompts and heuristics with a closed evolution loop: an audit agent runs the playbook, an evaluator scores findings against ground truth, and a reviser commits updates. The authors show evolved playbooks transfer the audit procedure to weaker agents, raising their vulnerability-discovery capability without retraining the underlying model. For defenders, this points toward scalable agentic auditing where procedural knowledge, not just model strength, drives results.

title: Curl will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026
blurb: Curl maintainer Daniel Stenberg announced the project will not accept vulnerability reports during July 2026, pausing its bug bounty program for the month to give maintainers a break from the volume of low-quality, often AI-generated submissions. Reports submitted during the window will be rejected and must be resubmitted in August, when normal triage and bounty payouts resume.

title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
blurb: ScarCruft (APT37), a North Korean state-sponsored group, is running a spear-phishing campaign that impersonates Microsoft Account security alerts to trick recipients into deploying NarwhalRAT. The lures play on account-compromise fear to drive execution, so defenders should flag inbound mail spoofing Microsoft security notifications and hunt for NarwhalRAT delivery artifacts on endpoints.

title: Measurement Study of Post-Quantum Readiness of Internet: 2026
blurb: A measurement study of 32,011 domains found that 49.3% now support hybrid post-quantum key exchange such as MLKEM768 with X25519, but 15.70% still negotiate only TLS 1.2, concentrated in banking and government. The authors show post-quantum adoption is uneven across sectors, leaving the most sensitive infrastructure least prepared for harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks. Defenders in lagging sectors should prioritize TLS 1.3 migration and enable hybrid key exchange before classical ECC and RSA become quantum-breakable.

title: The FCC Wants to Eliminate Burner Phones
blurb: The FCC proposed a rule requiring US telecoms to collect and store personal data on nearly all phone customers, including a government-issued ID number and physical address, effectively eliminating burner phones whose accounts aren't tied to an identity. The agency frames the mandate as an anti-scam measure, but privacy advocates warn it builds a centralized trove of identity data with significant surveillance and cybersecurity knock-on risks.

title: The Ghosts of Polymarket: When Off-Chain Matches Meet On-Chain Reverts
blurb: Researchers disclosed Ghost Fills, a class of attacks against Polymarket's hybrid architecture that matches orders off-chain but settles on-chain, where attackers exploit the timing gap to invalidate already-matched orders before finalization via four vectors (nonce bump, balance drain, allowance revoke, proxy trap). Across 1.95M reverted transactions they found 980,133 selectively reverted fills netting $1.49M and placing $1.78B at risk, with vulnerable contract code still present in 167 contracts across 10 chains holding $23M.

title: Software supply chains are heading for a transparency test
blurb: The EU Cyber Resilience Act will require manufacturers to create, maintain, and supply SBOMs once it takes effect in December 2027, pushing software bill of materials from best practice to legal obligation. ENISA's SBOM Adoption State of Play 2026 finds organizations preparing through SBOM tooling, automation, and development-process changes, with adoption skewing toward larger firms.

title: How Much Can We Trust LLM Search Agents? Measuring Endorsement Vulnerability to Web Content Manipulation
blurb: SearchGEO, a new evaluation framework, measures how attacker-published web pages get transformed into endorsed claims by LLM search agents, testing 13 backends across 308 manipulation cases each. Attack success rates ranged from 0.0% on Claude-Sonnet-4.6 to 31.4% on Gemini-3-Flash, and an agent-skill probe that turned endorsements into install commands split robust models sharply, with Claude over-rejecting while GPT over-trusted.

title: Teleport adds LLM Proxy and Delegated Identity to secure AI agent actions and access
blurb: Teleport added two capabilities to the public beta of Beams, its Agentic Identity Framework: LLM Proxy, which applies identity and access controls to the instructions an agent receives, and Delegated Identity, which governs and audits what resources an agent can reach. The features target the two points in an agentic workflow that currently lack auditability—what an agent is told to do and what it is permitted to access.

title: Binary Decompilation LLM with Feedback-Driven Multi-Turn Refinement
blurb: AutoDecompiler is a decompilation-specialized LLM trained with reinforcement learning that reframes binary decompilation as iterative multi-turn refinement, revising its output based on compilation, execution, and input/output testing feedback rather than generating a single pass. The approach targets a common failure mode in LLM decompilation, where generated code compiles and reads cleanly yet diverges from the original binary's behavior, misleading downstream vulnerability discovery and malware analysis.

title: 2021 Honda Civic infotainment system can be jailbroken via USB — flaw uses public Android test keys to install unauthorized apps, enables for 'EvilValet' attacks
blurb: Software architect Eric McDonald disclosed that the 2021 Honda Civic's infotainment head unit accepts USB updates signed only with a publicly known AOSP test key, letting anyone with brief physical access install arbitrary apps in an "EvilValet" evil-maid attack. The implanted code can use onboard mics, GPS, and cameras to record and exfiltrate data over Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, or cellular, though it cannot reach the vehicle's engine, braking, or door-lock systems.

title: SkillVetBench: LLM-as-Judge for Multi-Dimensional Security Risk Evaluation in Open-Source LLM Agent Skills
blurb: SkillVetBench is a public Hugging Face leaderboard that uses an LLM-as-Judge to vet open-source LLM agent skills, scoring them with SARS, a five-dimensional agentic-risk metric, alongside full CVSS v4.0 decomposition. The system caught all 78 confirmed-malicious skills with zero false positives across 22 benign controls, beating static scanners that miss 89% to 100% of instruction-layer threats like prompt injection and memory poisoning.

title: SoK: Taxonomizing the Low-Level Attack Surface of Modern Web Browsers
blurb: This paper systematizes the low-level attack surface of modern web browsers, mapping where memory-corruption flaws arise across input classes and components to expose regions underexplored by current fuzzing and testing. The authors structure the surface along input types and browser components, then chart how memory-corruption bugs cluster within that taxonomy. For AppSec and red teams, it offers a defense-oriented map of which browser subsystems warrant deeper security testing rather than continued effort on already-saturated targets.

title: obliv-clang: Real-World Oblivious Programming in C++
blurb: This paper introduces obliv-clang, a compilation-time tool that checks the obliviousness of C++ programs to defend against timing and access-pattern side-channel attacks on confidential data in trusted execution environments. It supports rich C++ features including arbitrarily nested pointers, formally proves the soundness of its checking rules, and aims to integrate with existing industry codebases while keeping compilation overhead and runtime performance costs low.

title: The Anatomy of Scam Scenarios: Large-Scale Characterization and Conversation-Aware Detection
blurb: This paper analyzes 102,054 real-world scam conversations to build a hierarchical taxonomy of 18 fine-grained scam scenarios grouped into 6 high-level tactics, characterizing the psychological techniques each scenario systematically deploys. The authors then use this scenario-level mapping to build a conversation-aware detection model for financial-institution customer interactions, flagging active scams mid-conversation so banks can warn and intervene before funds move.

title: MASCOT-Android: A Curated Dataset and Automated Collection Pipeline for Android Malware Source Code Specimens
blurb: MASCOT-Android is a curated dataset of Android malware source code paired with an automated framework that scrapes GitHub for new specimens, using repository documentation as the primary collection signal. A LinearSVC classifier trained on character-level TF-IDF features from README files alone distinguishes malware from benign repos at 96.28% accuracy and a 1.06% false-positive rate, with tunable confidence thresholds to balance coverage against FPR.

title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
blurb: Cisco patched CVE-2026-20262 (CVSS 6.5) in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, an authenticated arbitrary file write in the web UI that attackers are exploiting in the wild to overwrite files and escalate to root. Exploitation requires valid credentials with write access; defenders should patch to the fixed releases and audit /var/log/nms/vmanage-server.log for suspicious WAR file uploads.

title: Cybersecurity experts don’t think Anthropic’s Fable 5 presents a unique threat
blurb: Dozens of cybersecurity practitioners said Anthropic's Fable 5 offers no unique hacking capabilities, arguing recent jailbreak reports show nothing beyond what existing tools and models already provide. They called the US export controls on foreign use of Fable misguided, contending the restrictions target a threat the model does not meaningfully advance.

title: Di5Guise: 5G Privacy with vSIM
blurb: Researchers introduced Di5Guise, a privacy-enhancing 5G architecture that breaks the correlation between device identity and subscriber identity exploited by an operator-level adversary to deanonymize users across rotated profiles. Its core component, vSIM, is a virtualized SIM running on attested secure hardware that provisions a distinct, unlinkable device profile per subscriber, cutting re-identification accuracy from 93% to 49% when paired with traffic obfuscation on srsRAN-based 5G infrastructure.

title: Snyk VulnBench JS 1.0: Can LLMs Find the Same Bugs Twice?
blurb: Snyk's VulnBench JS 1.0 ran 300 repeated agentic LLM scans on identical JavaScript, prompts, and harness, and found that findings matching a Snyk Code reference were stable (134 of 158 appeared in all five reruns) while unmatched extra reports were noisy (80 of 161 showed up only once). Deterministic SAST systematically enumerated repeated data-flow sinks, so the authors recommend pairing agentic review with static analysis rather than treating either as a replacement.

title: Cardiac monitor maker's security skips a beat as data thieves go for the jugular
blurb: iRhythm Technologies, a California cardiac-monitoring firm, disclosed that attackers used social engineering against third-party business applications to exfiltrate protected health information and proprietary data, with an extortion demand received June 9 and the breach deemed material by June 10. The company says clinical systems and medical devices were unaffected and has not disclosed how many individuals are impacted, while no major ransomware group has claimed the intrusion.

title: SailPoint acquires AI agent security startup Entro for reported $200M
blurb: SailPoint acquired Entro Security, a Tel Aviv-based startup that inventories AI agents and the non-human identities they use, for a reported $200M. The deal extends SailPoint's Agentic Fabric product with out-of-the-box coverage for over 1,000 agent types and discovery of 1,200-plus non-human identity types, including API keys and tokens that agents rely on to reach external services.

title: Ent Security, which wants to build a new layer of workspace security that reads the intent behind what users and AI agents do, launches with $100M in funding (Duncan Riley/SiliconANGLE)
blurb: Ent Security launched with $100M in funding to build a workspace security layer that infers the intent behind user and AI-agent actions, aiming to flag malicious behavior that signature- and rule-based tools miss. The bet is that as autonomous agents proliferate inside corporate workspaces, defenders need intent-aware monitoring rather than activity logging alone.

title: A case for how to shape ‘ingredient lists’ for AI models
blurb: A policy paper from the Institute for Security and Technology argues that AI bills of materials (AIBOMs), inventories of the models, datasets, and tuning sources behind a system, need foundational standards before broad adoption to avoid fragmented, incompatible implementations. The authors, including former government SBOM lead Allan Friedman, target Hill aides and Pentagon staffers and call for parallel supply- and demand-side requirements to break the chicken-and-egg adoption problem.

title: Crooks found a new way to collaborate using Teams – by hiding command-and-control traffic
blurb: Attackers are using Microsoft Teams as a covert channel for command-and-control traffic, blending malicious instructions into the platform's API calls so they pass as legitimate collaboration activity. The technique exploits the trust enterprises place in sanctioned SaaS traffic, putting pressure on security teams to monitor Teams and other M365 services for behavioral anomalies rather than relying on perimeter or domain-based detection.

title: Attackers Exploit Three Fortinet FortiSandbox Flaws, One Patched Last Week
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting three critical FortiSandbox flaws (CVE-2026-39813, CVE-2026-39808, and CVE-2026-25089, all CVSS 9.1), enabling unauthenticated path traversal and command injection via crafted HTTP requests. Fortinet patched the first two in April 2026 and the third only last week, so IT teams running FortiSandbox, FortiSandbox Cloud, or PaaS deployments should apply the latest fixes immediately.

title: Cisco discloses second exploited SD-WAN vulnerability in two weeks (CVE-2026-20262)
blurb: Cisco disclosed CVE-2026-20262, a second actively exploited path-traversal flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager in two weeks, affecting all deployment types including on-prem, Cloud, and FedRAMP for Government. The bug lets an authenticated attacker with write access overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root, and fixed releases are now available across all affected versions.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Attackers compromised Awesome Motive's CDN and used a stolen API key to inject malicious JavaScript into the OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage WordPress plugins, which run on more than 1.2 million sites combined. The code triggered when an administrator loaded an infected page, harvesting authentication tokens to create rogue admin accounts and install a self-hiding backdoor that granted attackers full remote control.

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
blurb: Google released the final version of Android 17 and Wear OS 7, rolling out first to Pixel devices alongside a Pixel Drop that adds Gemini Omni video editing, Lyria 3 music generation, and AudioLM speech translation. The release doubles down on Google's strategy of using Android and Pixel as the showcase for its newest AI models, pressuring Apple ahead of its September Siri and iOS 27 upgrades.

title: Copilot 'SearchLeak' Attack Allows 1-Click Data Theft
blurb: Researchers disclosed "SearchLeak," a three-stage prompt-injection flaw in Microsoft Copilot that let attackers exfiltrate enterprise data through a single click using hidden URLs and crafted variables. Microsoft has patched the issue, but it joins a growing class of AI prompt-injection vulnerabilities that puts pressure on IT teams to vet Copilot deployments and agent-facing data flows before broad rollout.

title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-48172, an actively exploited privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel user-end plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and gave federal civilian agencies three days to patch under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The symlink-following bug lets attackers with FTP or web shell access escalate to root on shared CloudLinux/CageFS hosting, affecting all plugin versions before 2.4.8.

title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
blurb: ESET discovered Windows variants of the SprySOCKS malware family, which the Chinese threat group Earth Lusca used between 2023 and 2024 against government organizations in Taiwan, Thailand, Pakistan, and Honduras. The WIN_DRV variant adds kernel-level stealth, loading a driver signed with a leaked certificate to hide processes, files, and network connections, while supporting over 30 command-and-control commands across TCP, UDP, and WebSocket.

title: Rokarolla Android Trojan Levels Up to Full Device Control, Persistence
blurb: Zimperium zLabs researchers documented Rokarolla, an Android banking Trojan distributed through fake Chrome and TikTok downloads that targets 217 banking and crypto apps while using 137 commands to seize full administrative control of infected devices. Beyond credential theft, it harvests lock-screen passwords, logs keystrokes, blocks calls, deploys fraudulent overlays, and disables Google Play Protect, raising the stakes for enterprise mobile device management and BYOD policies.

title: One-Click Microsoft 365 Copilot Flaw Could Have Let Attackers Steal Emails, Files, and MFA Codes
blurb: Varonis Threat Labs disclosed SearchLeak, a now-patched flaw in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise Search that chained three bugs into a one-click path for exfiltrating emails, calendar data, indexed files, and MFA codes. Because the malicious link resolved to a legitimate microsoft.com domain, conventional anti-phishing and URL-filtering tools failed to flag it, leaving enterprises reliant on Microsoft's server-side fix rather than their own controls.

title: Google launches Android 17 and Wear OS 7, first on Pixel devices, with support for the latest AI models, a bubble bar UI, and live updates on Wear OS (Sarah Perez/TechCrunch)
blurb: Google released Android 17 and Wear OS 7, rolling out first to Pixel devices with support for its latest AI models, a new bubble bar multitasking UI, and live updates on the watch platform. The early Pixel launch sets the baseline for the feature set enterprise mobility teams will need to validate before Samsung and other OEM builds reach managed device fleets later this year.

title: Cybercrime Group Claims Novo Nordisk Hack
blurb: The hack-and-leak group FulcrumSec claimed responsibility for breaching Novo Nordisk, saying it used a GitHub access token to clone repositories, harvest further credentials, and exfiltrate 1.3TB of data including undisclosed drug programs, proprietary compound structures, and the Dicerna RNAi pipeline. Novo Nordisk says the stolen clinical-trial data was pseudonymized, but the group's failed $25M extortion attempt now threatens a leak that would expose core pharmaceutical IP.

title: CISA Flags LiteSpeed cPanel Plugin Flaw Exploited for Root Privilege Escalation
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-54420, a privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, giving federal agencies until June 18 to patch. The 8.5-severity bug lets a user with FTP or web shell access escalate to root on shared hosting servers running CloudLinux or CageFS, and admins should upgrade to LiteSpeed WHM Plugin v5.3.2.1 or higher.

title: Chinese Hackers Abused Google Workspace Rules to Steal Research and Defense Emails
blurb: A China-linked espionage group spent more than a year inside North American medical, academic, and military research networks, planting a credential-stealing backdoor on victims' REDCap servers and then rewiring their own Google Workspace mail rules to auto-forward sensitive research and defense email. The exfiltration technique abused trusted, native Workspace functionality, meaning IT teams that monitor for malware or external tooling would see legitimate mail-routing rules rather than an obvious breach.

title: China-Linked SprySOCKS Backdoor Expands to Windows with Driver-Based Stealth
blurb: ESET documented two Windows variants of SprySOCKS, a backdoor previously seen only on Linux and tied to the China-linked espionage group Earth Lusca, with one variant using a kernel driver to hide its processes, files, registry keys, and network connections. The malware supports over 30 commands and diverts TCP traffic through random ports to mask its listening port, raising the detection bar for enterprise endpoint and network monitoring tools.

title: Google Rolls Out Android 17; Major AI Features to Follow This Summer
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17, the latest version of its mobile OS, while its flagship AI features won't ship until later this summer. The staggered release lets Google get the platform update into enterprise device fleets now, though IT teams managing Android deployments should expect a second wave of feature changes and MDM policy updates once the AI capabilities land.

title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from social media starting spring 2027, requiring platforms including Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat, Facebook, and X to verify ages via ID upload or facial scans, effectively ending anonymous account creation for new users. Security and privacy experts warn the checks are easily circumvented and concentrate ID and biometric data in breach-prone systems, raising compliance and liability questions for any platform operating in the UK market.

title: iRhythm discloses data breach, says hackers stole patient info
blurb: iRhythm disclosed a data breach in an SEC filing after attackers used social engineering to exfiltrate patient protected health information from third-party-hosted business applications, then demanded a ransom on June 9 to avoid public disclosure. The company, whose cardiac monitoring service covers over 12 million patients, says clinical and medical device systems were unaffected and that it stores no payment or financial account data.

title: Cisco Releases Security Updates for Actively Exploited SD-WAN Manager Flaw
blurb: Cisco released patches for CVE-2026-20262, a medium-severity flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager (formerly vManage) that attackers have already exploited in the wild to overwrite arbitrary files and escalate to root. Exploitation requires valid credentials with write access, but IT teams running on-prem, cloud, or FedRAMP deployments should patch immediately and audit vManage server logs for suspicious WAR file uploads.

title: Microsoft faces down sueball, capacity problems in series of challenges
blurb: Microsoft faces a shareholder class action alleging it made misleading statements about Copilot adoption, after converting just 15 million of its 450 million Microsoft 365 users to paid seats and seeing its stock drop roughly 10% on the January 28 earnings disclosure. Separately, GitHub is reportedly tapping AWS for extra capacity to handle agentic-development load that Azure migration hasn't absorbed, raising reliability questions for large deployments like NHS England's 500,000-plus Copilot licenses.

title: Measurement Study of Post-Quantum Readiness of Internet: 2026
blurb: A measurement study of 32,011 domains finds that 49.3% now support hybrid post-quantum key exchange like MLKEM768 with X25519, but 15.70% still run TLS 1.2, concentrated in banking and government. The gap matters for IT leaders mapping crypto-agility timelines, since the laggards sit in exactly the regulated sectors most exposed to "harvest now, decrypt later" attacks and most expensive to remediate.

title: Databricks declares the end of pipelines with a unified platform for operational and analytical data
blurb: Databricks used its Data + AI Summit to introduce Lake Transactional/Analytical Processing, an architecture that unifies operational and analytical workloads on a single copy of data in the lake, eliminating the CDC pipelines, ETL jobs, and replicated databases that traditionally connect the two. The company is pitching it for AI agents that read and act on data in near-real time, with a paired analytics engine that promises millisecond responses without separate serving infrastructure.

title: Fake Microsoft Alerts Used to Deploy North Korean NarwhalRAT Malware
blurb: North Korea's ScarCruft group (APT37) is running spear-phishing campaigns that impersonate Microsoft Account security alerts, warning of "abnormal activity" and OTP abuse to trick recipients into opening a malicious ZIP attachment that deploys NarwhalRAT. The Python-based malware logs keystrokes, captures screenshots, records audio, and runs in memory via scheduled tasks, marking APT37's departure from its long-attributed RokRAT family.

title: AMD takes over MEXT for memory tiering tech that enables flash to appear as DRAM to applications — tech to 'address growing memory constraints' in the data center
blurb: AMD acquired MEXT, a startup whose AI-based memory tiering technology makes NAND flash appear as DRAM to applications, moving cold data to cheaper storage and prefetching it back before workloads request it. AMD plans to fold MEXT's Predictive Memory Engine into its data center portfolio, targeting memory-bound AI deployments where DRAM cost and capacity, not compute, increasingly cap how large workloads can run.

title: DocLang aims to make documents readable by AI, not humans
blurb: IBM, Nvidia, and Red Hat launched the DocLang working group under the Linux Foundation's LF AI & Data project to build an open, vendor-neutral document format designed for LLM tokenizers rather than human readers. The spec, joined by ABBYY and Human Signal and built on the existing DocLing toolkit, targets the cost and reliability problems enterprises hit when feeding PDFs and scanned files into generative AI and agentic systems.

title: Microsoft is moving Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and is considering a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a cheaper option (Ina Fried/Axios)
blurb: Microsoft is shifting Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and weighing a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a lower-cost model option. The pricing change moves Cowork away from flat per-seat licensing toward consumption billing, while a hosted DeepSeek would give enterprise buyers a cheaper alternative to OpenAI models inside Copilot without sending data to a Chinese provider.

title: Salesforce reels in customer support AI specialist Fin for $3.6B
blurb: Salesforce acquired customer-support AI vendor Fin for $3.6B, adding agents the company claims resolve roughly 75% of customer queries without human intervention. The deal strengthens Salesforce's Agentforce push into autonomous service automation and pressures rivals like ServiceNow and Zendesk competing for the same enterprise support-deflection budgets.

title: Software supply chains are heading for a transparency test
blurb: The EU Cyber Resilience Act will require manufacturers of products with digital elements to create, maintain, and provide Software Bills of Materials when it takes effect in December 2027, and ENISA's 2026 SBOM report shows most organizations have already begun building the tooling and automation to comply. Build-time SBOM generation leads at 39% adoption, though respondents still struggle to obtain SBOMs from suppliers, especially for commercial software products.

title: SMI's PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer SSDs coming next year, but severe NAND shortages will get even worse in 2027 as AI data centers swallow supply — An interview with Silicon Motion's SVP Nelson Duann
blurb: Silicon Motion will ship its first PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer drives next year, but warns that NAND shortages driving up SSD prices will worsen through 2027 as AI data centers absorb supply. The supply crunch already reshaped the market in a single quarter, lifting SMI's Q1 controller sales 105% year-over-year to $342.1M even as retail SSD demand fell on rising prices.

title: UK Regulator Weighs Cutting Data Center Power Use at Peak Times
blurb: Ofgem is weighing rules that would require new UK data centers to curtail power consumption during periods of grid stress, part of a plan to absorb surging electricity demand from AI and cloud workloads. Operators planning UK capacity would need to factor demand-response obligations into site design and capacity guarantees, adding a regulatory variable to siting and TCO calculations alongside energy cost and availability.

title: AMD’s massive SP7 socket for EPYC Venice and Intel’s gargantuan 9,324-pin socket for Diamond Rapids appear at Computex — SP7 and LGA9324-1 sockets will power the next generation of AI servers
blurb: AMD and Intel showed off their next-generation server sockets at Computex 2026, with AMD's SP7 supporting up to 256-core EPYC Venice chips, 16 DDR6 channels, 96 PCIe 6.0 lanes, and 1,400W peak power, while Intel's 9,324-pin LGA9324-1 will carry 192-core Xeon Diamond Rapids in 2027. The power and cooling demands point to mandatory liquid cooling and higher rack costs as enterprises plan AI-server refreshes around these platforms.

title: Salesforce acquires Fin, formerly known as Intercom
blurb: Salesforce signed a definitive agreement to acquire Fin, the customer-support agent vendor formerly known as Intercom, for roughly $3.6B, with the deal expected to close in Q4 of its fiscal 2027. The purchase extends Agentforce, which hit $1.2B in ARR last quarter, by adding Fin's Apex support model and fast-deploy packages aimed at SMB and commercial buyers who need quick time-to-value.

title: Windows 11 KB5094126 BSODing, freezing, forcing BitLocker lockout, breaks OneDrive, and more
blurb: Microsoft's Windows 11 Patch Tuesday update KB5094126 is triggering blue screens, system freezes, BitLocker recovery lockouts, and broken OneDrive and Dropbox access, though the company has not yet acknowledged the problems. HP machines hitting the 0xc0430001 BSOD appear tied to Secure Boot certificate changes, requiring admins to verify the boot.stl file is present on installation media before deploying the dynamic updates.

title: Cardiac monitor maker's security skips a beat as data thieves go for the jugular
blurb: iRhythm Technologies disclosed that attackers used social engineering to breach its business applications on June 8, stealing protected health information and proprietary company data before demanding payment to avoid public disclosure. The cardiac-monitoring vendor deemed the incident material and filed with the SEC on June 10, but has not named the threat actor or said how many patients were affected, and is relying on cyber insurance to absorb part of the cost.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: The Linux kernel 7.1 release will drop support for Intel's 486 processors, raising the minimum x86 baseline to Pentium-class chips with TSC and CMPXCHG8B instructions. The change simplifies kernel maintenance but forces the handful of organizations still running 486-era embedded or industrial systems to freeze on older kernels or migrate hardware.

title: ChapsVision to replace Palantir in major contract with French intelligence agency
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency DGSI ended its decade-long contract with Palantir, replacing the US data-analytics provider with French startup ChapsVision to reduce reliance on foreign technology for critical government services. The switch follows Germany's intelligence agency making the same move last month and lands days after Anthropic cut model access on US government orders, sharpening European fears of a "digital kill switch" on critical cloud and AI tools.

title: Microsoft’s Latest Surface PCs Offer Better Graphics, Faster AI
blurb: Microsoft refreshed its Surface Pro and Surface Laptop lines with new Qualcomm Snapdragon chips that deliver faster graphics and on-device AI performance, targeting creative professionals and AI power users. The update keeps Microsoft's premium hardware competitive against Apple's M-series MacBooks as enterprise buyers weigh Copilot+ PC fleets for AI workloads.

title: French Security Service to Replace Palantir With Local Firm
blurb: France's domestic intelligence agency, DGSI, will replace Palantir's data analysis tools with software from local firm Chapsvision. The swap reflects a broader European push to cut dependence on US tech vendors in sensitive government workloads, opening procurement opportunities for regional firms and pressuring Palantir's foothold in European public-sector accounts.

title: IT hurtles toward the ‘Great Enterprise Pricing Reset’
blurb: Gartner predicts at least 40% of enterprise SaaS spending will shift to usage-, agent-, or outcome-based pricing by 2030, with seat-based revenue share falling from 21% to 15% as AI tools pressure vendors to abandon per-seat models. The shift transfers forecasting risk from vendor to buyer, exposing IT leaders to budget volatility and invoice surprises from consumption-based billing they have not had to manage before.

title: Microsoft unveils new Surface Laptop with improved trackpad, Snapdragon X2, and more
blurb: Microsoft announced the eighth-generation Surface Laptop, powered by Snapdragon X2 Plus and X2 Elite chips with 80 TOPS NPUs for on-device AI, a haptic trackpad, up to 20 hours of battery, and a starting price of $1,599. The refresh holds the prior design but raises the entry price, pushing the mainstream Surface line further upmarket alongside the NVIDIA-equipped Surface Laptop Ultra.

title: Pentagon boasts of using AI to write reports mandated by Congress
blurb: The Pentagon's chief technology officer Emil Michael told a Hudson Institute event that the Department of Defense is using generative AI to draft its hundreds of congressionally mandated annual reports, cutting one report from 200 hours of staffing time to five. The tools run on GenAI.mil, the department's internal platform built on Google Cloud's Gemini for Government and available to all six military branches since December 2025.

title: Okta expands Google Cloud partnership to secure AI agents and the browser
blurb: Okta expanded its Google Cloud partnership with integrations that bring identity governance to AI agents and tighten Chrome browser security, connecting Auth0 for AI Agents to Google's Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform and adding Device Assurance signals to the Chrome Device Trust Connector. The move lets enterprises register agents in a central directory, tie each to a human owner, and route requests through Okta, addressing the gap where only 34% of organizations apply human-grade controls to agents.

title: Microsoft sued over performance of Azure business
blurb: Microsoft is facing a shareholder class-action suit from a Michigan pension fund alleging it misled investors about Azure's slowing growth and the costs of its AI buildout between May 2025 and January 2026. The complaint, which names Satya Nadella and Amy Hood, ties the disclosures to the $357B single-day market-cap drop after Q2 results showed Azure growth easing to 39% with weaker guidance.

title: HPE product barrage targets AI networks, agents, management
blurb: HPE used its Discover event to launch a wide AI-infrastructure package, headlined by the Juniper QFX5140 switch for AI inferencing and edge deployments, a QFX5252 module for its 72-GPU AMD Helios training racks, and an AI-native SASE platform. The release deepens integration between HPE's acquired Juniper portfolio and its existing AI data center gear, positioning HPE against Cisco and Arista for enterprise AI networking buildouts.

title: Marvell details vision of optically-interconnected data centers spanning across thousands of kilometers — new interconnects sampling later this year would allow CSPs to pool resources based on workload
blurb: Marvell detailed plans for coherent optical interconnects that would let cloud providers pool compute and memory across data centers separated by thousands of kilometers, with its Colorz 1600 1.6 Tb/s solution sampling later this year. The technology targets hyperscalers whose AI workloads have outgrown single facilities, positioning Marvell's optics and Teralynx Ethernet switches as the layer that turns multi-site campuses into composable infrastructure.

title: SailPoint acquires AI agent security startup Entro for reported $200M
